- The hidden power of not (always) fitting in. | Marianne Cantwell | TEDxNorwichED: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] thank you this talk is for those of us who are good at looking like we fit in piece of you never feels you 100% fit into any of them now if you've ever had an inkling of feeling that way you're not alone I mean I felt the same there are definitely two of us and I've spent the - Creating A Community And Finding Purpose | Stephen Jon Thompson | TEDxCarsonCity: Kind: captions Language: en I want you to take a moment and think about your ideal community. What does your ideal community look like? What does community mean to you? What if I told you my current job, my dream job, finding great engineering talent for companies like Google, Apple, and now LinkedIn relies heavily on personal connections and social media? You'd probably assume that was the case. But what if I told you that experience and that foundation started when I was 9 years old? My mother could no longer take care of me and my five sibs four siblings. So without a word or goodbye, she walked out of her weekly hotel room in a bad part of town forever. I was the eldest of five children. We ranged from the age of 8 to 1 years old. We sat in that hotel without food or water or power. We sat alone in that room dark and scared, hungry, and all alone. One year later I found myself in a jail cell in juvenile hall. I was alone again in the jail cell without my family. I sat in the cell reading books about other people's lives and I realized I wanted more. I stood up, I looked out this window, it was a gloomy overcast day, and I came to a painful realization. I was 9 years old and no one wanted me. and no one loved me. I was 9 years old. I was an incarcerated boy and unlike other children that were living their lives, I had come to experience the extreme world of poverty. Now, my my colleagues or cohorts were were probably playing uh Atari video games or um playing Little League baseball or even riding bikes. But me and my family, we were living in cars, hotel rooms, and the homes of strangers. We lived in shelters and abusive foster homes. I'd seen violence perpetrated against women, men being shot, and the police storming my grandmother's home to find our law-breaking mother. Me and my siblings endured a lack of attention from our mother, malnutrition, and no place to call home. I could barely read or write, and when I did attend school, I was deemed special ed. So in that jail cell, I started to feel normal. Yes, there was much older kids than I, but I wasn't scared. I had food and books and I was hungry for more. I felt like being told told what to do, getting three meals a day, created a routine and structure in my life that I was sorely missing. Sad as it will sound, for the first time in my life, I felt like I belonged. Ironically, that jail cell was more comfortable than any home I'd ever lived in. So in that jail cell, I made a promise to my 9-year-old self that I'd never be alone again and I'd find my family. One week late later I arrived at a place called the home. The official name was the Northern Nevada Children's Home and it was an orphanage. It was a place for kids like me. Like me, many of these kids had no families to support them. They were abused or in some cases in trouble with the law. The home was the last stop for kids before juvenile hall or long-term prison. Now, the home had seven cottages and there was seven or excuse me, 10 to 12 kids per cottage. And the home sat in the middle of this town only a stone's throw away from the state capital. For some of us, the home was just a pit stop from the maximum security prison a mere mile down the road. Prison loomed as a constant reminder of what was in store for many of us. There are 400,000 children in foster care in the United States. Approximately 23,000 18-year-olds graduate from this system every year. And the data talks about what happens to these kids next is absolutely staggering. One in five will be homeless at the age of 18. 71% of girls in foster care will be pregnant at the age of 21. Less than 3% will earn a college degree. Worse, one in four has experienced post-traumatic stress disorder twice the level of a US war veteran. So when I arrived in this town, I was a home kid and everyone knew it. This these overwhelming statistics along with the home was a backdrop to my life. But I reminded myself of the promise I made. Never be alone. From that day forward I would make a difference. So I arrived at my elementary school across the street from this very building and I started my journey. I was going to make friends with everyone I could. Over the next couple years I would make friends from all over town. I would build a huge base of friends. I made friends with kids from the home, kids from my school, my church, the basketball courts, music and dance events. I did whatever I could to make friends, and frankly, I was getting pretty good at it. Um but I found something in this networking and connecting, something I wasn't anticipating. And that was a community. And a community is basically a group of people with a shared connections that binds them together. In fairness, I had no idea that that's what I was doing. I was just making friends with my friends. I was you know, meeting their families. I was breaking dinner or breaking bread with them at their dinner tables. In some cases I was fortunate enough to share in their family holidays. But I was just a boy. I had nothing. And yet these people revealed their charity and friendship to me. It is something I wanted all along. So by creating a community from my friends, by nurturing these relationships with talks, games, church events, sharing in hopes and fears along with laughters and tears, I began to bond with these people. And the more I bonded with them, the more connected I felt to them. It was a call from a friend from their home. It was a knock on the door at their home. Even a ride home from school. It was those thousands of touches of those seemingly simple interactions that created oneness. I found that my life was probably bad bad compared to most. But I also found out that some people had it harder than me. Because of my own vulnerability due to abandonment, people were open to share their own faults and failures. The people in this community told me of the struggles of their families and how they were raised. I had friends that were valedictorians and friends that dropped out of high I had friends that were star athletes and friends that were not. I had friends that due to their families' wealth were able to travel around the world and I had friends whose families were living paycheck to paycheck. But no matter where they were from, I cheered for them. I loved to see my friends do great. I go to their recitals or plays, their award ceremonies. I did whatever I could to support my friends. I learned that everyone wants someone to be cheering in their corner. So at the age of 15, I found what I was looking for. Not to be alone. That day I got my second request and it was finding my family. So September 1986, a widow, a Mexican mother and her family took a gamble on me. This mother of two had nothing to gain by adding one more hungry mouth to her table. But her family welcomed me to their home. They taught me how to love the arts, not to be a taking a risk, and of course, how to make tamales. I learned a lot of things in this community. One of the things I learned when I was 14 years old was the state of Nevada offered a class to kids like me. It was a class on interviewing. It was a week-long course and it would take over a hot it would take over series over hot week in uh during the summer. So I took the course and I absolutely loved it. So it's only fitting that 30 years later, I live in Silicon Valley where my career centers around finding and connecting people, a skill I learned right here in this community. Today I help find data scientists who help explore the world of data. These data scientists help to solve fraud for consumers and they even design software for drones and wireless or driverless cars. But what I learned so many years ago was if I connected to people, I would always be bound to them. in this community, I found myself. In this community, I found my mission. And in this community, I found my family. My community has no borders, whether here in Carson City or in Silicon Valley. My community doesn't care if you live on the east side or the west side of town. My community doesn't care if you attend a mass or a synagogue. My community is my connection. I'm living my dream job and life today and I owe it to the people and my family here in this town. So thank you. It's been said It's been said that if you don't graduate from high school, get a college degree, you'll fail in your career or have no career at all. I contend that if you don't have a strong vital network, you will have nothing. My life has been based on really personal connections. When I wanted to go to college, I applied to this university and I applied to it twice. And both times I was declined. The third time, I reached out to my valedictorian friend who was attending the same university and I asked him for a reference. You can imagine how he must have felt to write a reference for someone his own age. But he did and I was accepted. I've benefited from my personal connections early in life as well as today. When I graduated from college, I moved to Lake Tahoe and I met this young lady. Later, she would introduce me to a startup company in Silicon Valley. That company was Google and I had the opportunity to go there and work alongside and support a woman by the name of Sheryl Sandberg who's now the COO of Facebook. I've had over the last 20 years hundreds of examples of how my personal connections have benefited me. But I've never had over that whole period one connection or one thing I did just by myself. Never. I you know, sometimes I get a question and it the question kind of bothers me but I understand where folks are asking. They say, "Steve, how did you overcome the odds?" And I I don't know how to respond to that because I assumed I was just like every other kid in this community. I had friends and I hung out with my friends. If my friends went to try out for football team, I would've tried out for a football team. If they went and got a summer job, I would've got a summer job. If they went and studied, I would've studied. If they had challenges in their lives, I knew challenges. My success is a symptom of my community. In an era of non-personal digital interactions where we can text anyone from our phone or where we can Google any answer on our phone, like that 9-year-old in that jail cell, we're all alone struggling to connect. So, we must build strong, viable networks and those networks aren't going to build themselves, folks. You're going to have to reach out and have coffee with a stranger. Stop at the corner store and talk to the lady that has an accent and ask her where she's from originally. Go to your local high school football game and even though you don't know any of the players, cheer like your son was playing. Go home and cook a meal for your elderly neighbor and then just sit down and talk to her for a while. We must connect to people. We must engage with people. Go home and call a friend. Send a friend a note on Facebook and if they don't reply, try again. See if you can volunteer at your local elementary school. And read, excuse me. See if you can volunteer and read at your local You never know what 9-year-old will be there excited to see you and eager to be your friend. So, I ask you again today, what is your ideal community? like? My hope is that I shared what my community looks like to me. Thank you.

money in the first place this extra is called interest and is a percent of the borrowed amount a savings account is a place where you put money that you intend on saving for a while and guess what you can actually earn a little extra money while your money is in the bank for real this is also called interest but instead of giving money back to the bank for a loan it is a little extra money that you earn while your money sits in the bank a checking account is another kind of bank account that you can use to pay bills either by check or buy a special card called a debit card checking accounts do not earn interest credit versus debit a credit card is a small hard plastic card that is issued by a credit card company or other financial institution they are basically a type of loan because using a credit card means you are borrowing money from the company who issued the card here's how it works the credit card company loans you money and you pay it back according to a schedule this is usually one payment per month with interest on the unpaid amount on the other hand debit cards are linked directly to your bank account which means you use your debit card to pay for things with money you - Economics for Kids: Saving and Spending: Kind: captions Language: en Saving and Spending &nbsp; Economics for Kids by Teaching Without Frills First, let's review. Many people earn money by working. Some people sell&nbsp;goods and others perform services to earn money. People use their money to&nbsp; buy the things that they need&nbsp;and want. But you can't have&nbsp; everything you want, right? Scarcity means that you&nbsp;want more than you can have.&nbsp;&nbsp; We all experience scarcity because we&nbsp; can want an unlimited number of things&nbsp;&nbsp; but we don't have unlimited money or&nbsp; resources, so we have to make choices. We usually buy things we need&nbsp; before we buy what we want.&nbsp; For example, I need to buy a healthy lunch, but&nbsp; I really want a cupcake. I should probably buy&nbsp;&nbsp; my lunch first and then see if i have&nbsp; enough money leftover for dessert. Sometimes we have to choose between spending&nbsp; our money now or saving it for later. All of the choices that we make about spending and&nbsp; saving have benefits and costs or pros and cons. When you spend your money you can buy something&nbsp; right away, but it might have to be something&nbsp;&nbsp; small that you can afford. And then you might&nbsp; not have much money left for other things. When you save your money, you may have&nbsp; to wait longer which is hard sometimes! But when you do, you can afford&nbsp; something more expensive. What would you do if you had five dollars? Would&nbsp; you spend it right away on an ice cream sundae?&nbsp;&nbsp; Or would you save it for a few weeks&nbsp; until you could afford a cool new toy? There's no right answer. We each just&nbsp; have to think about the benefits and&nbsp;&nbsp; costs of the choices that we make to spend or save. Chika, you can’t throw away your things just because they are slightly damaged. You must learn to love them and care for them. After all, they’ve been yours for so long and have given you a lot of joy. We can always sew another button on the jacket. And paint the car. But Chika didn’t listen. No! I don't want them. I want new ones. He put his old things aside and didn’t care for them anymore. One day, Chika saw his mother wearing a red dress. It looked very old. Chika saw that the dress even had a patch on it. Why is mom wearing that dress? It looks so old. Chika decided to ask his mother about the dress. Mom, why are you wearing that dress? You should throw it away and get a new one. Chika’s mother smiled. No, Chika. I love this dress. It’s my favorite one. It’s very special to me. Your daddy bought it for me soon after you were born. Really? Yes, Chika. And do you know when I wore it for the first time? When Mom? It was on your first birthday! Red was your favorite color when you were a baby. And you felt very happy whenever you saw me in this dress. And you would ask me to carry you each time I wore this dress. Chika’s mother told him more about the dress. Chika, do you know that when you first learned to speak, you would point out to this dress and say ‘red’. Red! I remember that now! Chika’s mother showed him pictures of his first birthday party. And he saw how pretty she looked in the red dress. Look at you, mom! You looked so pretty in the dress. And look at you, Chika. You were so chubby and cute. Just like a teddy bear. And look at how you’re holding my red dress here. Yes! Chika began to understand why his mother had kept the dress for so long. Every time I wear this dress, Chika, I remember all the good times we’ve had together. And it makes me love the dress all the more. I love you, mom! And I love this dress too! Please wear it again. Don’t ever throw it away. I won’t, Chika! That day, Chika learned to love all his clothes and toys no matter how old they were. And if they got a little damaged, Chika mended them and used them again. My car is as good as new! And I love this jacket. It has always kept me so warm.

- AFL Explained: Half Back Flank: Kind: captions Language: en The halfback flank is one of the most dynamic positions in modern football. Although it's true that all six defenders should employ a defend first mentality, trailblazers like Andrew Mloud in the '90s helped to shape the position into a counter punching asset. Today, positions during an AFL game don't often fall along traditional lines with teams preferring to describe positions by the role they perform for the team rather than where their magnet sits on a whiteboard. With that in mind, we can think of halfback flankers as the players who are most relied upon to transition the ball from defense to attack and launch attacking plays where they regularly abandon their direct opponent in a risk and reward-like approach. So, while at its core, the halfback flank is about balancing beating an opponent one-on-one and being able to launch attacking plays, players with different personal attributes and teams with varying tactics and priorities have given rise to several different types of halfback flanks in the modern game. We'll take you through some of these different play styles and what they contribute to their teams. Running rebounders like Adam Sard, Bailey Dale, and Daniel Rioli have become connoisseurs of a running gun style of play. They use all of their running, handballing, and kicking to link up for one twos and tech the opposition 50. Sitting behind the play allows these players to read the game in front of them, then pick their moments to explode up the field and launch their teams deep into attack. Teams who utilize these attacking halfbacks may often drop a seventh player behind the ball, creating a spare defender who can cover for their teammate when they take off down the field. Control-based players like Jackson Clair and Isaiah Wanging Meera who work in tandem at the Saints or Dane Zoro's role at the Lions utilize precise kicking to help shift lines and maintain possession. They're the sort of player who can be great to have in your fantasy team as they sit behind play, ready to receive the ball from teammates, wrecking up touches, and most likely taking more than a few kickins. The way they operate is similar to a basketball point guard, deciding the speed and direction their team plays, combining with other players for quick one twos and switches of play. During slower phases, when opposition teams drop back into their defensive structures, these elite kicking players will be relied upon to slice through a defensive zone. You'll also see halfbacks with a strong kick who have passed a teammate to receive a handball further up the ground, trusted to be the ones making the crucial kick inside 50 or launching a longrange shot at goal themselves. It can be difficult to define players to fit just one role. And in a halfback context, players like Nick Blakey, Tom Stewart, or even Luke Ryan at times can fit into these previous classes, as well as being able to play on taller opponents and execute more dedicated lockdown roles. Other players like Locky Whitfield can also sit partway between more of a controlling playmaker and a running rebounding halfback as the game or structure of the team requires. So, in short, there is no one correct way to play the halfback flank with a diverse range of players rolling out for different teams across the league. It's also not uncommon for players like Jack Sinclair to rotate between half back and the midfield throughout a game. Due to the position's damaging nature, recent years have also shown us times when a half back has been targeted by a defensive forward. Remember Nick Dayos and Ryan Clark's fiery encounter a few years back? or how Brisbane exposed the attacking nature of Blakey by sending Kamachi closer to goal in last year's grand final, lengthening the space between them and kicking four goals in the process. It can also be a useful position to develop midfielders, and we've seen players like Dos and Sheisel play here, getting the ball into their hands and giving them time to develop on field without exposing them to the physical demands of the midfield. For aspiring halfbacks at home, some important attributes to work on are your kicking in tight spaces, helping you to effectively set up play, and your repeat running efforts so you can work off your opponent and quickly relocate them in transition. Try and build trust with your teammates and other defenders, too, so that they'll get the ball into your hands at crucial moments, and be willing to come across and cover your opponent if you're called out of position. Do you want an aspect of AFL explained? What position should we look at next? Let us know in the comments and be sure to subscribe for more footie content.

- Halfback Blueprint Pt 2: EXECUTION & CONTROL: Kind: captions Language: en Most young halfbacks want to run the team, but they haven't even learned how to run a play properly. They try to control the game before they can control defenders. They want to be the general, but they haven't earned that rank yet. And this is where taking a structured approach to your training and development comes in. Now, this is part two of how to actually play halfback. And if you haven't watched part one yet, go back and do that first because everything we're covering today builds directly on top of that. In part one, we introduced the halfback pyramid, a three tier model that builds your game from the ground up. Tier one is close quarters, where you master everything in the immediate space around you, your individual skill set and your physical attributes. Tier two is the engagement zone, where you learn how to actually execute plays, run block shapes, sweep movements, and engage defenders with purpose. And tier three is command and control, where you manage the game, lead your team, and take full control of tempo and direction. We'll cover tier three in part three, so make sure you keep an eye out for when that drops. But in today's video, we're looking at tier two, the engagement zone. Now, just quickly before we get started, for those of you who don't know me, my name is Joe Murphy. I co-founded League Fit Academy nine years ago to help educate young rugby league players about how to build their body, their skills, and their mind. And we now work with thousands of players across six different continents right around the globe. Now, let's start by looking at tier two of the halfback pyramid or what we call the engagement zone. Okay, this is where you actually execute plays, block shapes, sweep movements, X players, anything and everything designed to engage the defensive line, manipulate defenders, and pull that defensive line apart. essentially trying to put opposition defenders under as much pressure as possible and causing them to make poor decisions and bad defensive reads that can lead to broken tackles, strong runs, line breaks, and ultimately try scoring opportunities. Now, before you start working on this area, you need to be sure you've got tier one covered because if your catch pass isn't up to scratch, it won't matter how well you run the play if you can't hit the receiver every time with a decent pass. But let's just assume that your catch pass is on point. You now need to run the shape that you're going to be using in a game. And in this context, when I say shape, I'm talking about running your part in the actual play. So, for argument sake, let's take what is sometimes called an X plus. An X plus is simply when you receive the ball, look to pull a runner, a runner underneath, you don't hit him, you dummy to him, and then you hit a receiver on your outside. Now, there's a ton of variations to this play, but we're just looking at a nice normal boring or vanilla flavored version of the play. When done like this, it's pretty straightforward and it's a very common play used from, you know, under tens right through to NRL level. But just because it's straightforward, it doesn't mean that it's simple. And here's the thing. In a minute, I'm going to be telling you that you need to be running this player down the park on your own. And you need to be getting in 20, 30, maybe even 40 reps to build up that muscle memory so that when you're in a game situation, you've got that really high level of both confidence and competence to be able to execute execute it perfectly every time. Um, but to get to that point, you're going to have to run an absolute ton of reps. But there's an old saying that's still floating around that says practice makes perfect. Now, this is wrong. incorrect, stupid. Right? Practice doesn't make perfect. Practice makes permanent. Um, and I'll say that again because repetition deepens the impression. Practice makes permanent. So, if you're practicing the play incorrectly, then when it comes to the game, you'll also run that play incorrectly. So, how do you fix it so that you can run the play the right way at both training and during the game? Well, there's three keys you need to remember when running any play. First, you must play straight. Second, get your spacing right. And thirdly, you need to communicate. Now, you need to make sure that you incorporate all three key elements because if even one is missing, the play will break down or fail completely. Think of it like a three-legged stool. Take away just one leg and the whole thing collapses. You're left on the ground flat on your back looking and feeling like a bit of a goose. Um, and if you leave out any one of these three, that's exactly what's going to happen whenever you try to run the play. So, let's have a closer look at each of them right now. Uh, key number one is to play straight. This is the biggest mistake a half can make, which is to drift across sideways, bringing the defense with you as you go. Playing straight means you need to square up. Square your hips up so that they're pointing straight up and down the field and not pointing at 45° out towards the sideline. What this means is that you need to run directly at defenders to hold them and fix them in place. This prevents defenders from sliding across the field and it opens up space for support runners. So let's have a look at what happens when you straighten up with the ball in hand. Uh this O is you. You receive the ball running an overs line and then straighten up. The defender you run at X now needs to make a decision. Does he straighten up on you as well? Because if he does, then that creates space on his outside shoulder. And these days, that's the exact gap that the lead runner in a block play is looking to run into. So, let's say he straightens up, but the defender on his outside shoulder doesn't. He keeps moving up and out. You as the half then hit the lead runner, or is it sometimes called the front door, and he goes through, the attack wins. Here's an example of this from Origin when Nathan Clearary hits lead runner Liam Martin who goes straight through. Now, if we run it again, this time the defender that's marking up on you doesn't see you as a running threat, and they don't straighten up at all, but rather keep sliding, which gives you as the half the opportunity to try to beat that a defender on his outside shoulder with your speed, which is often more than doable because that player is typically a bigger, slower forward. Again, attack wins. Here's an example. As Jamal Fog, he squares up to the defense, but the defense keeps sliding, allowing him to go through. Okay, so we've looked at squaring up and going yourself. If your opposite number doesn't square up, but rather continues pushing out, we've looked at hitting the lead runner in a block shape. If your opposite number does decide to square up on you, but the defender on his outside shoulder doesn't square up, leaving space for that lead runner to go into. So, what if your opposite number squares up on you and the defender on his outside shoulder also squares up on the lead runner in the block shape? What do you do then? Simple. You just go out the back to what's called the sweeper, the sweep play, or the back door runner. Any of those names. Because this is what you need to understand. In a nutshell, your main goal as a ball player is to take initiative and make the defensive line react to what you're doing. Once they react, you can take advantage of that. But you have to make them react. Taking that final example we just used where your opposite number squared up on you and the defender to his outside also squared up on the lead runner in the block shape where you pass the ball out the back to the sweeper. What are the options from here? Well, it really depends on how the rest of the defensive line reacts to the two defenders that have straightened up because if they keep sliding, then there'll be a gap right there that the sweeper for him just to run straight through. Attack wins again. Here's an example of the hammer doing just that for the Dolphins. Okay, but what if the whole Dline squares up? If this happens, even for a moment, space will have been created on the outside. Then it's up to the attacking side to take advantage of that space created. Here's the Tigers doing just that, going through the hands to finish it off in the corner. But all of this started because the half squared up. He straightened the attack and he forced the defensive line to make a decision. Or as as you you know you often hear rugby league commentators on the TV say they'll refer to someone like Nathan Clearary, Tom Didden or or Ponga and they'll say that player asks a lot of questions of the defensive line, right? So remember whenever you're running any kind of play, don't continually crab sideways, but instead square your hips up, run at a player, forcing him to make a decision. Be the half that asks questions of the defensive line. Now, the second key is to get your spacing right. And what that often means is usually to get deeper. Okay? There's nothing more common than seeing a side run shape and ball worker training look a million dollars doing it, but once they get into a game, it all breaks down. Now, yes, an opponent rushing up in your face will cause players to get things wrong. Um, so can the pressures of simply playing a game with family, friends, and a crowd watching, but this is why spacing is even more important. When pressure is applied in a game-like situation, depth often disappears, which takes away a side's ability to run that play properly. To give you an example of this, have you ever noticed how bands often play their songs faster live than they do on the actual recording? You know, that's adrenaline and momentum in action. When they're on stage, the energy is high, the crowd's buzzing, and even without meaning to, the band starts to speed up everything. Why? Because excitement and pressure make you want to go faster. It makes you want to rush things. Now, it's exactly the same with footy. um you get the ball, the Dline's charging out at you, your brain speeds everything up, and suddenly your spacing is off, your timing is off, and the play breaks down. A counter to this is to know that that's going to be the case and run your training reps 10 to 15% deeper than you otherwise would. That way, when you're in a game situation, that natural increase in, you know, excitement, energy, pressure will put you right where you need to be. So, always be mindful of your spacing. Now, the third and the final key is you need to communicate properly. Effective communication means that every player on the field knows exactly what they need to do within the structure of the play that you're about to run. Okay? And as a half, you're the one responsible for making that happen. If the play breaks down, that's on you. Um, now, we'll talk about this more in the next episode when we look at tier three of the halfback pyramid. But as a half, you need to understand that it's not enough to just call the play. You need to be the one orchestrating it. That means telling your lead runner where to go and reminding your sweet player how deep he needs to be sitting. Sometimes that's verbal. Other times it could be a glance, a nod or a hand gesture. But that communication has to be clear, sharp, and confident. Let's say you're running a basic block shape to the right. You've got a lead runner jamming in and a sweeper out the back and a center who's waiting for the ball. Before the play even starts, you've called the shape two plays ahead loud enough for everyone involved to get themselves into position. You point at your lead runner. Let's say his name's Henry. Henry, hit headgear's inside shoulder. He now knows you want him to run at the inside shoulder of the guy in the headgear. You glance at your full back out the back and you say, "Get deeper. They've been rushing us all day." He listens. He gets deeper. That two to three seconds of direction can be the difference between a play that works and a play that doesn't. So, communication isn't just about talking. It's about creating clarity so the players around you know exactly what they need to do. That's communication. So, here's the bottom line. You can't get good at this by just watching footy or running a play once or twice at training. You need to get the reps in over and over again. You've got to do it both on your own and with your team. You've got to do it at full speed and you've got to do it under pressure because it's only with repetition that you start to develop the timing, the instincts, and the confidence to execute it when it actually matters. And every single time you're practicing your shape, you must remember to play straight, get your spacing right, and communicate clearly. If you miss even one of these, the whole thing will fall apart. Now, if you haven't already watched part one where we broke down the close quarters game, go back and check that out because without that base, none of this works. And keep your eyes out for part three where we'll go in depth on the final tier which is command and control. And that is how you build your game the right way from the ground up. Trust me because I'm Joe and Joe knows.

Despite the swirling speculation about his retirement, insiders close to Cherry Evans confirm that he is not ready to hang up his boots just yet. At 36 years old, he plans to ink a one-year deal with the Sydney Roosters, signaling a new chapter after 15 solid years at Brookville Oval. While some pundits have expressed doubts regarding his move to the roosters, rugby league journalist Ben Dobin confidently dispelled these concerns, asserting that Cherry Evans remains committed to playing through 2026. All roads have been pointing to the Roosters, Dobin stated, quelling the retirement chatter that has surrounded the veteran. He insists that there has been no contract signed yet, but negotiations with the Roosters are hopeful. A contrasting opinion, the Daily Telegraph's Phil Rothfield urged Cherry Evans to consider retirement instead of taking the plunge with the Roosters. His concerns stem from the emergence of young talent Hugo Savala, who is currently on a development contract worth around $80,000. Rothfield fears that signing Cherry Evans could hinder Savala's promising future. As the debate continues, one thing is clear. The clock is ticking for Cherry Evans. The stakes are high as he navigates the twilight of his career. Will he forge ahead with the Roosters or will he heed Rothfield's advice to retire as a one club champion? The rugby league community is left on the edge of their seats, anticipating Cherry Evans next move as the future of both a legend and a rising star hangs in the balance.

- Is Panama Still the Best Place to Retire in 2026? 🇵🇦 Consider the Azuero Peninsula Before Deciding: Kind: captions Language: en-US Hey guys, Beasley Finds is back with another&nbsp; episode focused on the beautiful country of&nbsp;&nbsp; Panama. If you’re new to our channel, my wife and&nbsp; I are in search of amazing travel and retirement&nbsp;&nbsp; destinations, especially in Latin America.&nbsp; Panama has been at the top of my list for years,&nbsp;&nbsp; but is it still the best when it comes to&nbsp; retirement? In 2025 this country ranked number&nbsp;&nbsp; one on The Annual Global Retirement Index&nbsp; According to International Living Magazine;&nbsp;&nbsp; you can find a link in the description to this&nbsp; source. I tend to agree with their assessment,&nbsp;&nbsp; and I might be a little biased, but let me&nbsp; tell you why I think Panama still holds up,&nbsp;&nbsp; where you might consider living, and how to be&nbsp; strategic in your search for an apartment or house&nbsp;&nbsp; in Panama. And to satisfy the requests of some&nbsp; viewers, we’ll take a look at the map and explore&nbsp;&nbsp; the Azuero Peninsula of Panama- definitely a place&nbsp; worthy of your consideration. Let’s get going.&nbsp; The cost of living is usually a huge factor for&nbsp; many soon-to-be retirees and Panama has options&nbsp;&nbsp; for everyone from those with very modest budgets&nbsp; to those seeking more luxurious amenities. Right&nbsp;&nbsp; up there with cost of living is climate and&nbsp; lifestyle, and once again, Panama has something&nbsp;&nbsp; t o suit most people’s preferences. From hot and&nbsp; steamy coastal Caribbean towns to expat enclaves&nbsp;&nbsp; in the cool mountainous highlands, there is so&nbsp; much diversity in the landscapes and climates that&nbsp;&nbsp; Panama has to offer. This variety, and the freedom&nbsp; to choose from so many lifestyles is why Panama is&nbsp;&nbsp; still my top choice for retirement, especially&nbsp; for citizens of the United States and Canada,&nbsp;&nbsp; but also for many Europeans and citizens from&nbsp; other countries as well. Many of these travelers&nbsp;&nbsp; become expatriates in Panama or decide to fully&nbsp; immigrate and become citizens. These foreigners,&nbsp;&nbsp; from many countries, contribute to the unique&nbsp; fabric of Panamanian society. I have done several&nbsp;&nbsp; videos that focus on the more popular expat&nbsp; enclaves where many of these foreigners live so&nbsp;&nbsp; check out my Panama Playlist where I cover places&nbsp; such as Boquete, Coronado, and Bocas del Toro.&nbsp; I mentioned that Panama is especially attractive&nbsp; for American and Canadian citizens and this is&nbsp;&nbsp; because you can stay as a tourist (no visa&nbsp; required) for up to 180 days, about 6 months,&nbsp;&nbsp; with just a passport. Besides the U.S. and Canada,&nbsp; If you visit https://www.embassyofpanama.org/visas&nbsp;&nbsp; you’ll find a list of about 115 other countries&nbsp; with 3-month visa exemptions which allows tourists&nbsp;&nbsp; to stay for 90 days using only their passport. ese&nbsp; generous travel provisions mean that many people,&nbsp;&nbsp; from many countries, have the liberty to travel&nbsp; Panama and try before they buy. This is more&nbsp;&nbsp; important than you might think. For example, in&nbsp; the wake of the Covid-19 pandemic and even before&nbsp;&nbsp; this tumultuous time many Americans moving from&nbsp; the north and retiring to states such as Florida&nbsp;&nbsp; have moved back home or at least part of the way&nbsp; back to states such as North and South Carolina,&nbsp;&nbsp; Georgia, and Tennessee. This is such a prevalent&nbsp; phenomenon that the term halfback was coined to&nbsp;&nbsp; describe folks that move south to retire and end&nbsp; up moving half-way back home because they liked&nbsp;&nbsp; the idea of Florida much more than the reality&nbsp; of living there. The point is that living in&nbsp;&nbsp; Panama for half a year or even a few months is&nbsp; going to be invaluable when it comes to making&nbsp;&nbsp; a retirement decision, especially if you intend&nbsp; to buy. And not having to worry about getting and&nbsp;&nbsp; maintaining a visa right away is a really nice&nbsp; benefit. If you do decide to stay in Panama,&nbsp;&nbsp; the country offers many Visa types with the&nbsp; ever-popular retirement visa being of great&nbsp;&nbsp; interest to those with at least $1,000 of monthly&nbsp; pension income. With this relatively modest amount&nbsp;&nbsp; of money, you can retire in Panama and even become&nbsp; a citizen if you’re so inclined. Later in the&nbsp;&nbsp; video we’ll look at some important considerations&nbsp; for anyone pondering an extended stay in Panama,&nbsp;&nbsp; whether that be an apartment or a house. If you’re finding this video helpful and enjoy&nbsp;&nbsp; this type of content, please like and subscribe to&nbsp; support the channel. Now, let’s have a look at the&nbsp;&nbsp; map of Panama; I’ll note the ever-popular expat&nbsp; areas such as Panama City, Coronado, Boquete,&nbsp;&nbsp; and Bocas del Toro. These are the main towns and&nbsp; areas you’ll hear folks on Youtube talking about&nbsp;&nbsp; and they’re certainly worth exploring but I'd like&nbsp; to direct your attention to a region mentioned by&nbsp;&nbsp; one of my viewers who lamented that I haven’t&nbsp; covered the Azuero Peninsula. Our viewer isn’t&nbsp;&nbsp; the only one that has pointed out this region&nbsp; of Panama, with places like Chitre, Las Tablas,&nbsp;&nbsp; and Pedasi becoming more popular with foreigners.&nbsp; These are not big cities by any means and have&nbsp;&nbsp; smaller populations than areas like Panama&nbsp; City, Colon, or David but you can see from&nbsp;&nbsp; the map here courtesy of Thomas Brinkhoff at&nbsp; CityPopulation.de that the Chitre area has the&nbsp;&nbsp; biggest population and as you move your way down&nbsp; the peninsula, population and urban development&nbsp;&nbsp; thin out. Changing over to the World Settlement&nbsp; base layer on the CityPopulation website,&nbsp;&nbsp; we can see that Chitre is the most developed and&nbsp; has a population around 60,000 although I think&nbsp;&nbsp; the numbers in this dataset are a little low. We&nbsp; can see from this handy World Settlement Footprint&nbsp;&nbsp; map the areas shown in black are urbanization.&nbsp; You can check out a more advanced and useful&nbsp;&nbsp; version of this tool on the European Space Agency&nbsp; website that shows urbanization and development&nbsp;&nbsp; with color coding to indicate what year the&nbsp; development occurred in. Let me know in the&nbsp;&nbsp; comments if you need any help using this nifty&nbsp; tool and check for the link in the description.&nbsp; Continuing with our exploration of this&nbsp; area, the Azuero Peninsula is a Tropical&nbsp;&nbsp; Savannah characterized by grasslands with&nbsp; sparse forest and vegetative cover that,&nbsp;&nbsp; aside from the protected areas, has been largely&nbsp; cultivated for agricultural uses. For many&nbsp;&nbsp; Panamanians this is the heartland of their country&nbsp; and, from what I hear, the people are welcoming of&nbsp;&nbsp; expats and foreigners willing to integrate into&nbsp; the community. In other parts of Panama, like&nbsp;&nbsp; Boquete, I would say speaking good Spanish isn’t a&nbsp; necessity, but on the Azuero Peninsula, one should&nbsp;&nbsp; make a bigger effort. While it might not be the&nbsp; most gringo-friendly area, the more research I do,&nbsp;&nbsp; the more promising I find it, and I probably&nbsp; haven’t focused on this part of Panama because&nbsp;&nbsp; I am partial to the cooler Panamanian highlands.&nbsp; And while this region is very hot, it’s also drier&nbsp;&nbsp; than other areas such as along the Caribbean&nbsp; coast. It falls within the Arco Seco or Dry Arc&nbsp;&nbsp; of the country with rainfall totals much below the&nbsp; national average. Compared to the Caribbean Coast&nbsp;&nbsp; with annual rainfall totals of 50 to 150 inches&nbsp; per year the Azuero Peninsula only receives 40&nbsp;&nbsp; to 90 inches per year depending on the specific&nbsp; location. And there are plenty of microclimates&nbsp;&nbsp; in Panama so you can find something that is tuned&nbsp; to your preferences. And don’t make the mistake of&nbsp;&nbsp; thinking that the Caribbean side is the only place&nbsp; with nice beaches and a tropical vibe. There are&nbsp;&nbsp; many beautiful playas all along the coast of the&nbsp; Azuero Peninsula and even a little island or two&nbsp;&nbsp; like Isla Iguana Forest Reserve where you can&nbsp; spend a day relaxing on an uninhabited paradise&nbsp;&nbsp; island. If you’re feeling really adventurous, you&nbsp; might consider a tour of Coiba National Park- a&nbsp;&nbsp; much bigger island 90 minutes by boat from the&nbsp; mainland. Here you can snorkel or dive to see&nbsp;&nbsp; a huge diversity of marine life and go on nature&nbsp; hikes to see wildlife such as crocodiles, monkeys,&nbsp;&nbsp; and tropical birds. As you can see, the peninsula&nbsp; has much to recommend it. I’d say the only&nbsp;&nbsp; downsides are the heat, the distance from major&nbsp; cities, and lack of development. But the region&nbsp;&nbsp; is connected with Albrook airport near Panama City&nbsp; and, like most of Panama, is served by the Pan&nbsp;&nbsp; American Highway so I wouldn’t let the remoteness&nbsp; deter you from considering this location if it&nbsp;&nbsp; checks most of your other boxes. And for now, the&nbsp; cost of living here is very affordable with a low&nbsp;&nbsp; crime rate to boot. This may be an up and coming&nbsp; location worth investing in for those who don’t&nbsp;&nbsp; mind straying a bit from the well-beaten path&nbsp; in order to find the hidden gems. Overall, the&nbsp;&nbsp; Azuero is a great place to live if you’re looking&nbsp; for less rain, a slower pace, and proximity to&nbsp;&nbsp; great Pacific beaches and attractions. Now let’s move on to some strategic&nbsp;&nbsp; considerations when planning on a trip to Panama&nbsp; or an extended stay to scout some properties or&nbsp;&nbsp; rentals. Of course most planning begins on&nbsp; the internet these days. You can find a ton&nbsp;&nbsp; of resources online to help you get on target&nbsp; and start narrowing down places of interest.&nbsp; You can start your search for an apartment&nbsp; or house online but remember that nothing&nbsp;&nbsp; beats visiting the areas you’re interested&nbsp; in, and building a network of contacts.&nbsp; Some online resources are FB Marketplace,&nbsp; Encuentra 24, and Google Search&nbsp; Join Panama groups on Facebook and other&nbsp; social media if you haven’t already.&nbsp; Facebook groups have search functions&nbsp; where you can find posts with questions&nbsp;&nbsp; from people like yourself that may have&nbsp; been answered by the group already.&nbsp; Whether renting or buying, consider searching&nbsp; properties listed in Spanish because these could&nbsp;&nbsp; be priced substantially lower compared to English&nbsp; language listings geared towards foreigners.&nbsp; While not an official Multiple Listing&nbsp; Service the Panama Association of Realtors&nbsp;&nbsp; has a property search tool you can find at&nbsp; MLS® Acobir; link in the video description.&nbsp; An important way to stay on target in your&nbsp; search is to get super clear on the lifestyle&nbsp;&nbsp; and amenities that are most important to&nbsp; you. Do you want to live in an expat area&nbsp;&nbsp; like Boquete or are you willing to live more&nbsp; like a local on the Azuero Peninsula? What can&nbsp;&nbsp; you expect of these two different Panamas? 🏙️ With Expat-Style Living: you can expect&nbsp; Modern apartments or condos with new appliances,&nbsp; high-speed internet, english speaking staff,&nbsp;&nbsp; security guards, neighbors and landlords You may have, pools, fitness centers,&nbsp;&nbsp; and social areas; A/C in every&nbsp; room and hot water at every faucet&nbsp; Newer building amenities may also include&nbsp; backup electric generators, water cisterns,&nbsp;&nbsp; rooftop swimming pools, on-site restaurants&nbsp; and cafes, and co-working spaces to name a few&nbsp; You should expect higher&nbsp; rent: from $1,200–$2,500/month&nbsp; You’ll pay more but you’ll have more&nbsp; comforts, conveniences, and peace of mind&nbsp; 🏘️ With Local-Style Living: you can expect Simpler apartments or houses, with less&nbsp;&nbsp; English-speaking neighbors, and fewer frills While security staff are often part of the&nbsp;&nbsp; package in expat-oriented rental situations,&nbsp; don’t expect security services outside of&nbsp;&nbsp; these areas unless you’re in a gated community. May not be A/C in some buildings (especially&nbsp;&nbsp; at higher elevations) and hot water&nbsp; may not be available at every faucet&nbsp; But you will find significant cost savings in&nbsp; more local areas, so you can spend more money&nbsp;&nbsp; on the things that matter to you so long&nbsp; as you don’t mind making some sacrifices.&nbsp; Besides lifestyle considerations, the choice&nbsp; between renting or buying will determine your&nbsp;&nbsp; costs and overall experience of living in another&nbsp; country. If you still haven’t decided between&nbsp;&nbsp; an apartment or a house, that’s okay because&nbsp; you can do both. But before you do anything:&nbsp;&nbsp; Make sure the person offering to rent&nbsp; or sell the property actually owns it.&nbsp; There is a national land registry&nbsp; in Panama, but not all property is&nbsp;&nbsp; titled so verification is critical Invest in a Panamanian lawyer for&nbsp;&nbsp; peace of mind in all of your transactions Now let’s look at how renting and buying are&nbsp;&nbsp; different from countries like the U.S.&nbsp; and Canada, and how these differences&nbsp;&nbsp; might inform your living abroad strategy. With Renting: the theory is the same in Panama&nbsp;&nbsp; but it’s application tends to be a bit different For example, many rentals and even houses come&nbsp;&nbsp; furnished which is not typical in the U.S. And apartment complexes are often owner-operated&nbsp;&nbsp; buildings as opposed to being run by a management&nbsp; company, which is great when you have an owner&nbsp;&nbsp; who cares and solves problems quickly, but&nbsp; not so great when the opposite is true.&nbsp; And although there are fewer tenant rights&nbsp; in Panama there is more opportunity to move&nbsp;&nbsp; around considering the availability of&nbsp; short-term rentals such as AirBnB and&nbsp;&nbsp; that many units come furnished which makes&nbsp; moving from place to place much simpler.&nbsp; With Buying: There are definitely some differences&nbsp; in the process to consider because Panama’s real&nbsp;&nbsp; estate market is not as structured and&nbsp; streamlined as it is in many countries&nbsp; For example, Property financing&nbsp; isn’t widely available in Panama&nbsp;&nbsp; and many deals are made in cash. However, there are several banks&nbsp;&nbsp; such as Banco General and Scotia&nbsp; Bank that will lend to foreigners&nbsp; Check the link in the description for a&nbsp; guide on purchasing property in Panama&nbsp; Many properties are listed online but there&nbsp; isn’t a well-established MLS like in the U.S.&nbsp; Some properties include furniture and appliances&nbsp; and may be listed as for rent or for sale&nbsp; Buyers and sellers work with real estate agents,&nbsp; but licensing standards are less strict for real&nbsp;&nbsp; estate professionals so don’t expect everyone&nbsp; that works in the business to have a license&nbsp; Foreign buyers have the same property&nbsp; rights as locals, but having an attorney&nbsp;&nbsp; is strongly recommended for navigating&nbsp; the legal and economic system in Panama&nbsp; Paying for a home may be done through escrow,&nbsp; but buyers also make direct payments to sellers&nbsp; Buying in Panama comes with many cost savings, not&nbsp; least of all is that property taxes are much lower&nbsp;&nbsp; in Panama than the U.S. and new constructions can&nbsp; qualify for a complete tax exemption for 20 years.&nbsp; So, as we’ve seen, Panama really continues to&nbsp; stand out as a retirement destination. Whether&nbsp;&nbsp; you’re drawn to the conveniences of expat-friendly&nbsp; communities, the tranquility of the highlands,&nbsp;&nbsp; or the slower-paced authenticity of&nbsp; regions like the Azuero Peninsula,&nbsp;&nbsp; there’s truly something here for&nbsp; everyone. The country’s diverse climates,&nbsp;&nbsp; accessible visa options, affordable cost of&nbsp; living, and welcoming culture make it an easy&nbsp;&nbsp; place to imagine calling home—whether&nbsp; for a season or for the long haul.&nbsp; I hope you’ve enjoyed this episode of&nbsp; Beasley Finds. If you want me to dig&nbsp;&nbsp; deeper into anything we covered today just drop&nbsp; a comment below. And if you found this helpful,&nbsp;&nbsp; please give it a like, subscribe, and check out&nbsp; other videos in my Panama Playlist. Thanks for&nbsp;&nbsp; spending some time with me today and I’ll see you&nbsp; in the next episode of Beasley Finds- take care.

  1. @beasleyfinds [9 likes]: *UPDATE* 
Claim: "...new constructions can qualify for a complete tax exemption for 20 years."
Fact: This is outdated information. Under Law 66 of 2017 (implemented in 2019), the 20-year exemption was phased out for new residential permits.
New Rule: Properties with a value of $120,000 to $300,000 (registered as a "Family Patrimony") generally receive a 0% tax rate indefinitely. For values above that, or for non-primary residences, exemptions are now typically scaled to 5, 10, or 15 years depending on the specific project registration date and type.
Primary Source: Dirección General de Ingresos (DGI) - Panama Tax Authority

belittle the plight of those poor players so they became less compassionate yes and i'm buying it and these are patterns that we're observing consistently over hundreds of different individuals you see people who are winning because of the flip of a coin start to act as if they actually deserve to win looks like i made some good investments one of the questions we ask is why did you win the game and you could imagine that rich players would say well i won the game because of that flip of the coin i had two times the advantage but none not one of the rich players attributed their inevitable success in this game to that force of luck that randomly got them that privileged position in the first place when we watch patterns of human interactions people who feel entitled and deserving of their own success are more willing to privilege their own interests above the interests of other people and often engage in ways that undermine other people's welfare so that they can get ahead and - CHALLENGE RATING | The ONE Thing You Need To Know: Kind: captions Language: en No one: Challenge rating! Yes, of&nbsp; course, I use it as intended to&nbsp;&nbsp; prepare combat encounters and calculate the&nbsp; appropriate level of difficulty for my party,&nbsp;&nbsp; always accounting for their resource&nbsp; expenditure during a given adventuring day! Me like 4 years ago: Oh, CR! Yeah,&nbsp; totally Critical Role has been such&nbsp;&nbsp; an inspiration for me when I DM,&nbsp; and I'm trying to use the what&nbsp;&nbsp; Matt does to really just bring out&nbsp; the best in my game and my players-- So challenge rating or CR is that little&nbsp; number near the bottom of the most important&nbsp;&nbsp; information on a stat block. It's meant&nbsp; to give you an at-a-glance look at the&nbsp;&nbsp; difficulty rating of a monster, but there's&nbsp; a lot of behind the scenes to those numbers&nbsp;&nbsp; that you can use to really calculate optimal&nbsp; encounters for your group! Using CR can be&nbsp;&nbsp; anywhere really from an art to a science,&nbsp; and wherever you fall on that spectrum,&nbsp;&nbsp; in this video we're going to break down&nbsp; what CR really is, and how you can use&nbsp;&nbsp; challenge rating most effectively for your&nbsp; game with some tools to help you do so! And&nbsp;&nbsp; the ONE thing you must remember whenever you're&nbsp; using CR to design encounters! Let's do this! Hey, Bob here :) and welcome to Bob World Builder&nbsp; where we learn how to have more fun playing D&D&nbsp;&nbsp; together! So hit that subscribe button for new&nbsp; videos every Wednesday under your subscriptions&nbsp;&nbsp; tab! Now the Dungeon Master's Guide has some&nbsp; very math-heavy pages about the four levels of&nbsp;&nbsp; encounter difficulty, XP thresholds, multipliers,&nbsp; modifiers, budgeting for an adventuring day,&nbsp;&nbsp; but you don't need to use most of that stuff!&nbsp; I'd bet this is the first time a lot of you&nbsp;&nbsp; are hearing about it! And while I've read that&nbsp; section, I've never actually used those pages&nbsp;&nbsp; to calculate optimal encounter difficulty for my&nbsp; game, and I've been having fun playing D&D for a&nbsp;&nbsp; couple years now! But in those several pages of&nbsp; tips for setting encounter difficulty, there is&nbsp;&nbsp; ONE box of text about CR: what it actually is and&nbsp; how you should use it! So let's break that down! "When putting together an encounter or&nbsp; adventure especially at lower levels,&nbsp;&nbsp; exercise caution when--" Hold up! "Exercise&nbsp; caution 'especially?'" No, no. 'Exclusively'&nbsp;&nbsp; at lower levels! There should be no caution at&nbsp; the higher levels because encounter balance goes&nbsp;&nbsp; against a lot of what D&D should be! An immersive&nbsp; world where anything can happen, and your party&nbsp;&nbsp; will need to think their way out of just as many&nbsp; encounters as they can fight their way out of!&nbsp;&nbsp; Balancing encounter difficulty is really just&nbsp; a matter of trying to keep your party alive,&nbsp;&nbsp; and in the first few levels of the game (maybe&nbsp; level 1 to about 4 or 5), it is pretty easy&nbsp;&nbsp; for characters to die. So at level 1 when your&nbsp; party can get squashed by just about anything,&nbsp;&nbsp; it's good to use CR. And this&nbsp; is understandably very tricky&nbsp;&nbsp; because if you're a brand new DM running&nbsp; for new players and no one knows what CR is,&nbsp;&nbsp; here's this other thing you have to learn about&nbsp; this whole game system you're trying to wrap your&nbsp;&nbsp; head around! If that's you, we'll talk about&nbsp; an easy way to do that in just a second! But&nbsp;&nbsp; in my opinion trying to balance encounters beyond&nbsp; level 5 is just very counterintuitive to what D&D&nbsp;&nbsp; is meant to be! Just lean into what is likely&nbsp; to happen in that type of environment and what's&nbsp;&nbsp; going on in the story that your players need to&nbsp; know about! Should you choose to use CR throughout&nbsp;&nbsp; your gameplay, it should be one of the last things&nbsp; on your list when coming up with encounters. "...exercise caution when using monsters whose&nbsp; challenge rating is higher than the party's&nbsp;&nbsp; average level." Here is some simple, actionable&nbsp; material and it's the way that I think most DMs&nbsp;&nbsp; actually use CR! Look at the stat block. It's a CR&nbsp; 4 creature. That means if you have a party of four&nbsp;&nbsp; level-four characters, it should be&nbsp; a pretty fair encounter for them.&nbsp;&nbsp; If you have two monsters of that same challenge&nbsp; rating, now it's really sliding into the monster's&nbsp;&nbsp; favor! Even adding a CR 1 creature with that&nbsp; CR 4 one will make things pretty significantly&nbsp;&nbsp; harder for your party! And yeah, there's plenty&nbsp; of nuance to really doing this correctly which&nbsp;&nbsp; we'll cover in just a minute, but first here's&nbsp; why you need to look at that average party level! "Such a creature might deal enough damage with a&nbsp; single action to take out adventurers of a lower&nbsp;&nbsp; level. For example, an ogre has a challenge&nbsp; rating of 2, but it can kill a first level&nbsp;&nbsp; wizard with a single blow." Bingo. It's really all&nbsp; about just giving these super squishy characters&nbsp;&nbsp; a chance at survival! So when you're looking at&nbsp; a stat block and the challenge rating seems okay:&nbsp;&nbsp; it's within one level above or&nbsp; below your average party level;&nbsp;&nbsp; it's still really important to look at the&nbsp; damage it can deal in a single action. If the&nbsp;&nbsp; maximum single action damage is higher than the&nbsp; characters HP with the highest HP in your party,&nbsp;&nbsp; you probably don't want to use that monster! Or&nbsp; you can just change their damage dice to something&nbsp;&nbsp; a little bit lower. And many creatures&nbsp; even at these levels have multi-attack,&nbsp;&nbsp; meaning they'll be doing not one but two attacks&nbsp; of that same type per turn; thereby killing&nbsp;&nbsp; two characters in one turn and super likely&nbsp; spiraling toward a TPK! And like I said at the&nbsp;&nbsp; top of the video, this is all about giving those&nbsp; squishier characters, especially at level one,&nbsp;&nbsp; a chance. But there's a whole style of play where&nbsp; people like those first level characters having a&nbsp;&nbsp; tough time! So leave a comment below about&nbsp; whether or not you think those squishy guys&nbsp;&nbsp; really do deserve a chance! And if you've noticed&nbsp; how even starter modules can be really unbalanced,&nbsp;&nbsp; check out my guides on the Dragon of Icespire Peak&nbsp; for help balancing all of those encounters! This&nbsp;&nbsp; summer I'll be publishing pdf guides of each&nbsp; of those videos on my Patreon, just like I'm&nbsp;&nbsp; doing now for Rime of the Frostmaiden! Liking,&nbsp; commenting, subscribing, and joining Patreon&nbsp;&nbsp; are all great ways to support the channel, and me!&nbsp; So thank you! But now let's get back to the text. "In addition some monsters have features that&nbsp; might be difficult or impossible for lower level&nbsp;&nbsp; characters to overcome. For example a rakshasa has&nbsp; a challenge rating of 13 and is immune to spells&nbsp;&nbsp; of 6 level and lower. Spellcasters of 12th level&nbsp; or lower have no spells higher than sixth level,&nbsp;&nbsp; meaning that they won't be able to&nbsp; affect the rakshasa with their magic,&nbsp;&nbsp; putting the adventurers at a serious&nbsp; disadvantage. Such an encounter would&nbsp;&nbsp; be significantly tougher for the party than&nbsp; the monster's challenge rating might suggest." So this tells us that CR can be a great indicator&nbsp; of a monster's difficulty, but it's really&nbsp;&nbsp; important to read the whole stat block! That&nbsp; last line about challenge rating being kind of&nbsp;&nbsp; misleading is basically a subtle admission of this&nbsp; one thing you need to remember: challenge rating&nbsp;&nbsp; has no context! ~no context!~ There are just far&nbsp; too many variables that the makers of this game&nbsp;&nbsp; could have ever tried to wrap into one single&nbsp; number: the number of enemies, the environment&nbsp;&nbsp; and what's going on around the party during&nbsp; this combat, are they fully rested or not, do&nbsp;&nbsp; they have magic items including health potions--&nbsp; that is a huge way to turn the tide of a battle!&nbsp;&nbsp; CR is good for the mechanical side of D&D that&nbsp; needs to be kind of streamlined for adventurers&nbsp;&nbsp; league play, but D&D always was and will be&nbsp; primarily a game for your friends and your family&nbsp;&nbsp; at home! So when designing encounters, focus on&nbsp; what's fun! What are some cool monsters you've&nbsp;&nbsp; recently read about that you want to incorporate&nbsp; in your game? If they're in a desert or an arctic&nbsp;&nbsp; wasteland or a forest, they're going to probably&nbsp; see very different monsters in those kind of&nbsp;&nbsp; locations! And if they're in a forest, what's the&nbsp; likelihood of running into a green dragon anyway?&nbsp;&nbsp; Probably pretty low! There can't be that many. And&nbsp; something like a dragon or many of the big cool,&nbsp;&nbsp; just spectacular monsters in the monster manual&nbsp; are things that will really affect your story! So&nbsp;&nbsp; really you should just be considering what kind&nbsp; of monsters you want your party to experience&nbsp;&nbsp; and why, not what a number tells you to do!&nbsp; Again after about level five when a bunch&nbsp;&nbsp; of your characters have multi-attack, CR is the&nbsp; last thing you should be looking at when planning&nbsp;&nbsp; a cool encounter, but when you're in tier one or&nbsp; you just really wanna make sure they're going to&nbsp;&nbsp; be okay during that adventuring day, use Kobold&nbsp; Fight Club! It's a totally free website that&nbsp;&nbsp; does all the math on those pages in the Dungeon&nbsp; Master's Guide for you! But still, don't get too&nbsp;&nbsp; obsessed with it because those numbers are not&nbsp; going to determine what's fun for your players!&nbsp;&nbsp; Funny enough, those same pages in the Dungeon&nbsp; Master's Guide then include a section about ways&nbsp;&nbsp; to design fun encounters with things that&nbsp; will totally throw off the CR calculation!&nbsp;&nbsp; But only YOU know their fears and their flaws and&nbsp; their backgrounds and their desires of what they&nbsp;&nbsp; really want to achieve as that character! And what&nbsp; will really make for a fun session! So remember&nbsp;&nbsp; to give this video a thumbs up if you found it&nbsp; helpful, and thank you to all the new patrons&nbsp;&nbsp; like Katherine, Daniel, Davi, Vili, Dylan, Aaron,&nbsp; Calvin, The Mustard Tiger, Jimmy, Shane, Dale,&nbsp;&nbsp; Syforce, Jason, Luke and Highlander Wins! Thank&nbsp; you for all your support, and keep building! :D

  14. @thalia.tenebris: My father always told me that CR means that a four-person party of that level will lose a fourth of their resources while beating that creature.
(He has been dming for over 30 years.)

For example, a CR 3 Green Hag (without a coven) could be baten by an average 4-person party, but a fourth to half of their resources (spell slots, potions, hp, etc.) will be expended.

Edit: You can also use CR calculators online. There are even ones that take multiple creatures added up into account.
  15. @dantestrider3578: Thank you! I'm planning to start dming soon, but the two games I've played, my squishy lvl 1 character got demolished and I really hated that, so I want all the tools I can get (besides a deus ex machina or two in my sleeve) to avoid my players experiencing that
  16. @Ccagesniper: Everyone forgets to calculate the experience pool of the monsters. 

A monster that gives more experience at the same CR is harder than one that gives less experience. I really like using tools that calculate the experience based on the number of players and their levels and gives me an at a glance idea of how hard it's going to be. It tends to be fairly accurate and allows me to make multiple encounters on the fly per session with out thinking too much about it. 

Just looking at the CR of a creature alone is the completely incorrect way of calculating encounter difficulty and not how the balancing of the system was even designed.
  17. @mikfhan: If total hp after long rest < 16 then add the difference as temporary hp, gives a buffer to avoid random wipes early on so you can run more encounters and short rests.

Meaning, it is not as limited as we think it is. Because now this entire system is based on technology. Trading, data, payments, everything is digital and where there is technology, there is a huge danger hidden. The threat of cyber attack and now this threat has taken a new form. Where Artificial Intelligence i.e. AI itself has started finding the weaknesses of the system. This is where the Securities and Exchange Board of India ( SEBI) enters. Which has recently issued an important warning here. A warning has been issued by SEBI stating that AI based tools like Mythos are now developing so rapidly. It is developing itself so that it can identify the weaknesses and vulnerabilities of the system on a very large scale. And if this thing goes into wrong hands, then it can become a big threat for all the financial institutions. Now let us try to understand it in a little easier language. Earlier, finding flaws in the system used to be a very long and technical process. But now AI can scan thousands of systems and find their vulnerabilities in a short time. And if a hacker gets this information, he can take advantage of these vulnerabilities and steal data. The entire system can be attacked by hacking it or disrupting the service. And since the entire ecosystem of the stock market is interconnected. That is why the problem in one place can spread rapidly to other parts as well. Also called the cash scraping impact here [music]. Now, considering this entire risk, SEBI has made cyber security. A task force called AI has been created. Which includes exchanges, registrars, transfer agents and other market participants. And its whole motive is not only to identify the danger but also to prepare a way to deal with it. Such as sharing threat intelligence , providing timely information about cyber attacks and securing the entire system in a coordinated manner. SEBI has clearly stated in its advisory that now companies should not depend only on the old securities system. Rather, along with AI-based tools, it has now become necessary for them to take steps like regular vulnerability checks, timely system updates, patching, strong API security and continuous monitoring. Apart from this, SEBI has also advised to adopt advanced models like Zero Trust Architecture [Music]. In which every access is verified here. Be it from inside or outside. Another important point here is that just securing your company is not enough because sometimes the threat can also come through third party vendors or service providers. That is why SEBI has also said that companies should strengthen the security standards of their partners and vendors [music] and also conduct their risk assessment regularly. Apart from this, it is also advised to connect with the Market Security Operation Centre so that the threats can be tracked in real time and any important actions can be taken immediately because AI driven cyber attacks can spread very fast and the scope of delay in them is very less. If there is even a slight delay, the loss can be huge. So if you try to understand the whole issue, then on one hand AI is making the technology stronger and on the other hand AI is also increasing the cyber threats manifold and SEBI now wants to keep the entire financial system safe by being prepared in advance so that any small weakness does not turn into a big problem.
