- How to Actually Play Halfback: Kind: captions Language: en Most young halfbacks don't develop the way they should. And the number one reason is simple. They never take a structured approach to building their game. They watch the NRL, see a playmaker throw a cut out or hit a perfect block shape, and they think, "Yeah, I can do that." But they skip steps. They jump straight into trying to call set plays and run the team before they can even consistently hit a target with their pass. And here's the truth. You don't learn to run the show by looking like a halfback. You learn by building your game like a halfback. And that starts with what we call the halfback pyramid, which is a three- tier model that builds your game from the ground up. Tier one is your close quarters game. This is where you master everything in the immediate space around you. Your catch pass skills, kicking, footwork, and of course, a strong running game. This is your foundation, and it's both your individual skill set and your physical attributes. That's tier one. Tier two is the engagement zone. This is where you actually execute plays, block shapes, sweep movements, X plays, anything and everything designed to engage the defensive line, manipulate defenders, and pull that defensive line apart. Essentially trying to put opposition defenders under as much pressure as possible. And finally, we have tier three, which is command and control. This is where you run the team. You call the sets, you control the tempo with using your kicking game, and you generally lead the side around the park. But here's the catch. You can't lead a team if you can't run shape. And you can't run a shape if you can't throw a clean pass and hit the target with the right timing and the right execution. There's no shortcut for that. Okay? You build your game from the bottom up or you don't build it at all. Now, because this is such a big topic, we're going to break it up into two parts. So, today we're going to be looking at tier one, which is the base or the bottom layer of that halfback pyramid, the foundation on which your whole game needs to be built. Now, just quickly, for those of you who don't know me, my name is Joe Murphy. I co-founded League Fit Academy 9 years ago to help educate young rugby league players about how to build their body, their skills, and their mind. And we now work with thousands of players across six different continents right around the globe. So, we just mentioned there's three tiers or three layers to the halfback pyramid with each tier building on and requiring the mastery of the tier below it to work properly. Tier one is your close quarters game, tier two is the engagement zone, and tier three is command and control. And we'll be looking at tiers two and three in the next video in part two. Right now, let's get stuck into tier one, which is your close quarters game. And this is far and away the most important tier because everything else a halfback does must come off the back of this. Think of it like this. In the military, close quarters battle or CQB for short. It's high speed, high stakes combat in tight, enclosed environments where reaction time is minimal, and the difference between success and failure is milliseconds. It's about what's happening in your immediate surroundings right now. At this level, there's no time to think. Every move, every shot, every step is the result of skills that have been drilled relentlessly until they become almost instinct. Okay? CQB is about executing a refined, highle skill set under extreme pressure. Not by thinking about it, but by reacting with precision and control and relying on that highly trained skill set to get the job done. Whether you're talking about Navy Seals, the SAS, or even a police SWAT team, these operators train relentlessly so that when they inevitably find themselves in these hostile situations, they have the required skill to get the job done. Now, as a halfback, if you want to get as good as you possibly can be, then you need to take a similar approach to train your close quarters game relentlessly. And this really comes down to two main categories. You need to work on both your skill set and your physical attributes. Now, those physical attributes we're talking about, those are things like speed, strength, acceleration, change of direction, and a great change of pace. You need to be building a fast and agile physique, but you also need to make sure that you have enough physical resilience to handle getting hit by much bigger and stronger players. And if that's not enough, you got to be fit enough to perform your job just as well in the last minute of the match as you do in the first, keeping in mind that over the course of a game, the halfback probably talks more than the rest of the team combined. And you can't do that if you're gassed. So being super fit is a non-negotiable. Okay. Now, those physical traits, they're all things you need to work on firstly in the gym with your strength and your power development and secondly on the actual footy field where you do all of your speed, acceleration, change of direction work as well as your conditioning. So those are your physical attributes. Now, the other aspect or the other area you're going to be working on as a halfback is you need to have a very particular set of skills. A very particular set of skills which like those Navy Seals or special operators, you must train relentlessly so that those skills can be performed without you even having to think about it. This is what we call unconscious competence. Now, unconscious competence is essentially when you've put in so much work that you can perform the skill and I mean execute it perfectly and you can do it without even thinking about it. Now, how long does it take to get to that point? Well, you know, it differs for everyone and natural talent definitely plays into it. But to get to the point where you're unconsciously competent, where you can execute the skill 99 times out of 100, you're going to have to put in a ton of boring, monotonous work um so that you can move through the other three levels of what we call the skill acquisition continuum, okay? Which is essentially the process of of acquiring and mastering skills. Now, the first level of that is what's called unconscious incompetence. This is where you suck, but you don't even know it. Okay? This is often what it's like the first time you're exposed to any new skill. You simply don't know what you're doing. Okay. Now, the second level is conscious incompetence. This is where you suck, but at least now you know you suck. Oh no, we suck again. Right. Um, this is when you know what the skill should look like, but also you know that when you perform the skill, it just doesn't look right. Why doesn't mine look like [Music] that? Why? That's conscious incompetence. The third level is conscious competence. This is when you can execute the the skill correctly some of the time you know what it should look like and when you're really concentrating, you're really focused. Sometimes you get it right and sometimes you don't. Okay, that's that third level. And the fourth and the final level is what we want to get to which is unconscious competence. You can now execute the skill whenever you want in whatever circumstances without even really thinking about it. And this is the goal to get your skills to this point. A great example of this is learning to drive a car. Okay, first time you get behind the wheel, you're super nervous, you're concentrating as hard as you can, and you're probably making lots of mistakes, and that's normal, right? Um, this is unconscious incompetence. But if you keep practicing, then by the time you take your test, hopefully you've progressed to the point of being consciously competent. You can drive a car safely as long as you're focused on what you're doing. But once you've been driving for a few years, you can probably jump in the car to drive home, the radio's playing music, you're chatting to a mate next to you, and you still drive home safely without even having to think about it too much. Okay, that's unconscious competence. That should be the level that you're looking to get all of your skills to. Now, specifically, what skills are we talking about here? Well, we're basically talking about everything. So, you know, early catch, grip, carry. We're talking about your passing game. So, 6:00 passes, long range spiral passes, pop passes off your hip when running an X shape, the up and over harbor bridge style pass to an unmarked winger that's becoming a bit more popular lately. You've got the fast early catch and release in one movement type pass which is closely related to the tip on players like um you know Latrol Mitchell are so good at using to put their centers and wingers into space. We're talking about both your short and your long kicking game and even your ability to shift the ball effectively from one side of the body to the other to be able to fend off defenders and create space. Now, how much work do you need to put in to acquire this level of skill? But simply put, a lot, right? Uh, performing a spiral pass for 20 minutes three times a week is definitely better than nothing and you'll improve, but it's not as good as training that spiral pass for 20 minutes seven days a week. Kobe Bryant, regarded by many as one of the top two or three greatest basketball players of all time. He would often practice a single shot, not for minutes, but for hours at a time. He'd pick one spot and one shot and he'd stay there until he made that shot 400 times. Not attempted, made. If he missed a shot, he wouldn't count that. He'd keep going until he made 400. Uh, because to Kobe, it wasn't about reps. It was about mastering the skill. It was about developing that skill so that when he needed it, it was there. Now, I could go on and on. Yes, skill acquisition does take a different amount of time for different players because natural ability, it massively plays into it, right? But the point is you need to put in whatever amount of skill is required to get your skills to the unconscious competence point. Now, why are these two things your physical attributes and your skills as or as I referred to them earlier, your close quarters game, why is that at the base of the pyramid? And why is your whole game built on this one aspect? Because there's no point using elaborate set structures if you can't execute a simple catch and pass. If you can't put the ball where it needs to be, there's no point running an X shape. You know, if every time you look to pull someone underneath, they drop the ball because you deliver an absolutely rubbish pass. There's no point trying to create indecision in the defensive line or opening up a chance to run, if you're too slow, too fat, or unfit to take advantage of the opportunity. So yes, there's definitely more to it and we'll look at those other tiers of the pyramid, the engagement zone and command and control in the next episode. But right now, the best thing any young halfback can do is to take a structured approach to their training and start by building their close quarters game. Focus on both your physical attributes and your core skill development, and you'll lay the foundation, the bottom tier of that halfback pyramid that sets you up for long-term success. Trust me because I'm Joe and Joe knows.

Donc je faisais le coup de pied et je continuais jusqu'au bout. Et à chaque fois que je le faisais, c'était génial, mais je ne dirais pas que mon coup de pied est génial. Regarde les Crusaders. Lamar se fait charger à l'épaule, se plie et je le frappe peu près à cette hauteur du sol et je me souviens que tout le monde rigole. Donc ne me suis pas. Mais bon, j'ai vécu des situations vraiment amusantes. Dan Carter. L'année dernière, les All Blacks avait l'habitude de faire des coups de pied gênants avant la Coupe du Monde. Et tu sais, je l'ai pris au pied de la lettre. Comme, salut, tu donnera un coup de pied au but à un joueur de pied droit ? Comme ça serait assez cool. Il a dit, "Ouais, je le ferais. Je me suis dit, "Eh bien, tu dois essayer d'en faire une. Tu sais, avant que tu finisses. Je vais essayer. Si nous étions en finale de la Coupe du Monde et que nous menions de 20 points. Et le pire, c'est qu'il l'a fait en finale. C'est comme si personne ne le savait, car nous celebrons le score de personne. Et après le match, ils se précipitent vers moi et il me dit "Qu'est-ce que tu fais ?" Les gars, j'ai botté avec le mauvais pied. Il l'a fait. Donc, c'est une histoire que j'ai trouvé intéressante, c'est celle de Dan Carter. Il est le gars le plus détendu, rien ne le dérange. Et qui fait ça lors de la finale de la Coupe du Monde. Ton dernier coup de pied de ta carrière. Donc, beaucoup de mes followers, y compris moi-même, ont connu des moments similaires. En fait, le fait de ne pas être sélectionné dans des équipes et de devoir prendre la décision de continuer dans le rugby ou d'explorer d'autres options. Alors, je suppose que la dernière chose que je veux conclure, c'est quel a été votre moment ou vos moments difficiles, ou les équipes avec qui vous avez échoué et où vous avez dû prendre une décision, vous dire : "Je dois m'y mettre à fond. J'y suis arrivé assez rapidement. J'ai quitté l'école et joué au rugby en club. Je pensais m'entraîner vraiment dur. J'ai eu un essai pour l'équipe des moins de 20 ans, qui était une équipe très compétitive avec des joueurs comme Zac Guildford, Sam Whitelock,... Tous ces futurs All Blacks. Et honnêtement, ça a été un choc pour moi. Maintenant, avec du recul, je me rends compte que j'étais vraiment en-dessous de mon potentiel. Pas assez affûté et pas assez agressif en défense quand je les percutais. J'ai raté les moins de 19 ans et j'étais plutôt déçu. Je suis rentré chez moi, j'ai joué en club, je me suis cassé l'appendice, c'était vraiment triste. L'année suivante, j'ai réussi à jouer et à me former, ce qui était vraiment génial. Mais je pense que j'ai eu de la chance. Je me suis entrainé avec un coach et il a beaucoup cru en moi et m'a donné une chance. Mais j'ai joué deux ans en équipe de la Coupe et je n'ai pas pu intégrer la Super Rugby. C'était juste un pas de trop. Je n'ai même pas pu obtenir de contrat plus important, même si je pensais bien jouer. On m'avait promis, au début, de cette équipe que les Hurricanes me prendraient pour une place plus importante. Mais à la fin, ils m'ont dit que je n'étais pas assez rapide, que je n'avais rien amélioré par rapport à ce que nous avions prévu et qu'ils ne me sélectionneraient pas pour l'équipe, ou plutôt, ils avaient un programme de trois sélections qui consistait à participer à la pré-saison. Ce qui était vraiment éprouvant. J'étais désespéré. J'ai alors décidé de poursuivre ma carrière de coiffeur. J'avais 20 ans, je venais de remporter la Coupe du Monde des moins de 20 ans. J'avais manqué le rugby de haut niveau et je me demandais quoi faire. Ma mère et mon père m'ont dit de rejoindre l'équipe aller de pré-saison et de donner encore un an. Tu es encore jeune, profite de cette opportunité. Alors j'ai repris l'entraînement et c'est là que j'ai vraiment retrouvé ma forme physique. Je pesais environ 85 à 86 kg à l'époque. Maintenant, je flotte. Je commencerais bien par un régime très bas, mais à l'époque, j'étais descendu à environ 60 kg. J'ai réussi à perdre environ 3 kg, passant à environ 84 kg. Et puis je suis descendu à environ 81, 82 kilos. Je courais 175 mètres en 45 secondes, puis je l'ai amélioré à 66 mètres en 16 secondes, ce qui était rapide pour moi. À partir de là, mon jeu a commencé à s'améliorer. J'ai beaucoup travaillé sur ma défense et mon jeu au pied. Et tout a fini par se mettre en place cette saison-là. Mais tout cela était dû à un changement de régime alimentaire, à la prise de compléments alimentaires, à un suivi quotidien de mon poids, à une consommation abondante d'eau et à la planification de mes gâteries. Je prévoyais quand faire la fête et quand manger chez McDonald's. Et cela a changé ma vie. J'ai eu une période de six mois où j'allais courir. C'était incroyable pour moi. J'ai connu une très bonne année pendant un ou deux mois. C'était mon moment où on m'a dit que je n'étais pas assez en forme et qu'on ne me choisirait pas. Je trouve cela incroyable car chaque joueur qui a réussi a dû fournir un énorme travail acharné. Toi, tu a eu une période de six mois. Pour revenir sur cette période de six mois, just pour finir, à quoi ela ressemblait-il concrètement au quotidien ? Parce que cela me frustre d'entendre les anciennes générations parler de surentraînement. Je crois qu'on peut se surmener musculairement, mais on peut s'entraîner à améliorer ses compétences, travailler différents groupes musculaires et différentes hoses chaque jour, en intensifiant l'effort pendant 6 mois. Ce qui permet d'obtenir une augmentation musculaire importante. Alors, à quoi cela ressemblait-il concrètement ? Pendant la saison morte, je faisais beaucoup d'exercices. J'ai commencé à courir sur route. On m'a conseillé de faire 6 km en environ 30 minutes. Je faisais cela trois fois par semaine. Mais la chose que je faisais chaque semaine, le lundi, le mercredi et le vendredi, c'était une séance d'entraînement. Pas trop intense, mais régulière de 30 min environ. Nous courions jusqu'au stade FMG, montions en haut, puis revenions. Nous brûlions juste du gras et cela m'aidait à perdre des kilos en trop. Alors j'ai demandé : "Que puis-je faire d'autre pour m'entraîner ?" J'habite à côté du stade FMG. Et il m'a dit : "Ne fais pas ça avant le petit-déjeuner." Alors nous y allions, 6 ou 7 fois, pendant 1h. Nous montions et descendions tranquillement, juste pour nous mettre en forme. Mais mon poids a diminué. Pour moi, tout a changé. Quand j'ai perdu du poids avec mes jeans serrés, le jeu est devenu plus amusant. Mais pas comme... Je pensais à, comment je me sentais pendant les matchs, ce n'était pas amusant. Je faisais mon footing à 7 heures, puis je mangeais des œufs, des omelettes blanches, ensuite je partais travailler chez Forsch. Donc je rentrais chez moi. Oui. Je restais ici toute la journée. Mon père m'aidait et je déjeunais juste pour me reposer. C'était bien d'avoir cette routine. Le soir, on allait à l'entraînement au club et voilà. C'était bien, mais l'entraînement à l'académie était intense. Donc, après cette séance d'escaliers le matin ou de boxe, nous avions des séances de musculation. Donc, tu sais, la rigueur de l'académie, tu ne gagnes pas d'argent et tu t'entraînes aussi dur mais tu le fais dans l'ombre. Ce furent des moments amusants. Je ne prends plus de pause parce que les dommages que cela cause sont difficiles à réparer.

- How to Control a Rugby League Game as Halfback: Kind: captions Language: en Most young halves say they want to lead their team around the park. They want to run the side. They want to be the little general. But here's the truth. You don't get what you want, you get what you work for. Because you know what? Everyone wants those things. But wanting doesn't make it happen. The will to win is not nearly as important as the will to prepare to win. And if you want to learn how to control a rugby league game as a halfback, then it's going to be hours of preparation that make the difference. time spent on specific deliberate training aimed at improving your skills, your physical preparation, on developing the ability to engage the defensive line, and last but not least, developing the command and control side of the game where you learn to manage the game, to lead your team, and take full control of the tempo and direction of your site. If you do that, you'll take your game management to a whole new level, and you'll put yourself in the position to not just control the game, but dominate it. And in this final tier of the halfback pyramid, that's exactly what we're talking about, command and control. So today, we're going to be going through tier three of the halfback pyramid. And I'm going to be sharing with you the one training tactic that if used properly will bring everything else together. Now, for those of you who don't know me, my name is Joe Murphy. I co-founded League Fit Academy 9 years ago to help young rugby league players build their body, their skills, and their mind. And we now work with thousands of players all over the globe. From juniors in under 12elves right through to players preparing to go into their first full-time NL preseason. And over all those years, one thing has become clear. There's a massive difference between a half who just plays some good footy and a half who runs the show. And that difference is what tier three is all about. What we call command and control. But what does that mean exactly? Well, first those are military terms. So let's start by looking at it from a military perspective. First, command. The person in command is the one who's in charge. They make decisions, assign missions which are designed to achieve a particular outcome. They allocate resources and essentially they're the one that's held accountable for outcomes. Okay, that's command. Now, control. Control is more about the actual execution. It refers to the systems and procedures and communications used to carry out the commander's intent effectively. Good control ensures units are coordinated, information flows up and down the chain of command, and people can adapt to changing situations. So command is essentially leadership and decision-m and control is organization and communication, right? So how does this apply to rugby league and more specifically to you as a halfback? Well, when we're talking about command and control in the context of the halfback pyramid, you're the commander on the field. So command means you take ownership of the team's direction. You call the shots. You decide where the play goes. You identify any weaknesses you see on the field like a fullback who's out of place or a winger who's not wide enough and he's left some space on the outside or maybe there's a middle forward who's gassed out. He's out on his feet and he's a liability in the middle of the field. As the half, you need to see those things and make sure the team takes advantage of them. Okay, that's command. Now, control. Control is how you actually make that happen. It's how well you communicate. It's how you organize your forwards. It's how you get everyone on your team to the right place at the right time and make sure that the set flows the way that it's supposed to. It's when you see, you know, that opposition middle forward that's gassed out on his feet like we mentioned earlier and it's not just recognizing that as an opportunity, but having the skill set to make adjustments to what your side's doing to take advantage of that opportunity, right? So that's control. So command is taking ownership of the team's direction and control is how well you can organize and communicate to make that happen. So, what that means for you as a halfback who's looking to be a game manager and a leader on the rugby league field is that A, you need to make a decision about what your side's going to do, and B, you need to effectively communicate that decision to your team and then lead and organize them to actually do it. And achieving this comes down to two things. It's about knowing what to do and about how to go about doing it. We call that knowledge and application. One without the other is useless. It's no good knowing the set structure that you're supposed to be using if you're too timid to yell at your teammates to get them into the right position to run the plays that they're supposed to be running and have the set play out the way it's supposed to play out. So often you see halves blame the players around them for not getting into the right shape to run a play. But when you speak to those other players, they didn't even know what that play was that they were supposed to be running. Why? Because the half didn't communicate with them. And on that point, let's have a little bit of a side note here. you standing on the field looking at the rock and then half turning your head and yelling at a yelling a play over your shoulder. That's not communicating. All right? If the players you're communicating with haven't clearly heard your instructions, understood those instructions, and then started to act to follow those instructions, then the communication has broken down on your end, not theirs. So, let's look at a definition to make this crystal clear. To communicate means to share information clearly and effectively so that someone else understands it and can act on it. If they don't understand it, then you didn't communicate well enough with them. So knowledge and application is knowing what you need to do and how to go about doing it. So let's start by talking about the knowledge side of things. Okay. Now, what do you actually need knowledge of? To be a good halfback, you need knowledge of three things. First, you need to know your team structures, as in the different types of sets that you can run, and just as importantly, when to run them. Second, you need to know the individual plays that exist within those set structures themselves. And for each and every one of those plays, you've got to know who should be where, and then when they need to be there by. Uh, and thirdly, you need knowledge of your teammates. You've got to know their strengths, their weaknesses, where they fit into the individual plays themselves, as well as the team structure as a whole. Now, I'm going to repeat it because repetition deepens the impression. You need to know your team structures, including when to use them. You need to know the individual plays that exist within those set structures. And you need knowledge of your teammates. That's the knowledge that you need because the best communication in the world means nothing if you've got nothing valuable to communicate because as the halfback you need to be across every single detail. You need to know those three areas as much if not more than the coach because the coach isn't going to be on the field with you, right? It's up to you to take control and lead the site around. And this is where your communication comes in. Now, we spoke about this earlier about what communication means, which is you've said something, they've heard you say it, and they've understood what it is that you're really asking them to do, and they've acted on it. Okay, that's communication. Now, this is so important because it's the one thing to have the knowledge about how, say, your set should work. It's another thing entirely to get the 12 other players in your side to run that set properly. So, here's three simple steps to help making communicating with your teammates much more effective. Step one, you need to know who gets it and who doesn't. At training, pay attention. You'll start to notice which players pick things up quickly and which players just kind of not along but still really don't know what's going on. And as the halfback, that's important because the way you communicate to player A, who knows the shape and positioning is very different to how you communicate to player B, who constantly gets timing wrong or forget who he's running off. Communication isn't just barking orders. It's about adjusting your message to the person you're delivering it to. Step two, direct your message clearly and repeat if necessary. If you know someone's struggling to get the timing right on a sweet player training, you don't just need to yell at them. Get deeper and move on. You've got to find a way to get through to them. Break the movement down. Show them where to start from. Get them to walk it through. Explain the line they're supposed to be running and repeat it until it clicks. That's what good communication looks like. Not just giving directions, but making sure those directions actually land. And trust me, if you're doing this at training, leading your teammates through set plays and ball work, all while staying patient, then by the time you actually get out onto the field, your team will trust your voice, follow your direction, and they'll look to you when it matters most. And that's what leadership is about. Now, step three is to use your teammates to help. You can't micromanage every player on the field, and that's not your job, but you can delegate. If one of your ed edge forwards, let's say, understands the system really well, use him. Get him to talk to the center or the winger outside him, get him to lead that edge. You're still in charge, but now you've got lieutenants helping you carry out the plan. Look, and look, most teams have a few players that they're a bit of a space cadet, off with the fairies, however you want to describe it. They might be good footballers, but they're not good at remembering where they're supposed to be during sets. But by using the more switched on players that are around them, um, you can help get them in the right position. Okay, that's control. That's smart communication, and that's leadership. And once you learn how to do this properly, you'll start to notice something. You don't have to yell as much, and you don't have to overexlain because your play your players start trusting you. And that is what command and control is really about. Knowing your stuff, communicating clearly, and making sure the whole team is pulling in the same direction. Right? So, we've talked about command and control and how command is essentially leadership and decision-m and control is organization and communication. We've also talked about how they can be further broken down into knowledge and application with knowledge referring to you needing to know your team structure, the individual plays that exist within that structure and that you also need solid solid knowledge of your teammates, of their strengths and their weaknesses. And that finally brings us to application. The first component of which is communication and I just gave you three steps that you can use to make your communication better. That was the first component of application. the second component of application and what turns out to be the key to not just the third tier of the pyramid but the entire pyramid itself. It's the one thing that will truly elevate your game as a halfback and it's the one thing that will take you from being just a handy football player to becoming a genuine leader where every part of your game starts to come together. That thing is what we call shadow ball work. So let's jump into that right now. Okay. So what is shadow ball work? Well, it's a rugby league variation of a boxing method called shadow boxing. Shadow boxing is where a fighter throws punches in the air with no opponent um to practice technique, footwork, rhythm, and strategy. It's been around for over a century and become a staple of uh in boxing training because it allows fighters to refine their technique, mentally rehearse fight scenarios, and build coordination and timing. Legendary boxers like Muhammad Ali and Mike Tyson help make shadow boxing famous. Often seen moving around the ring, visualizing their opponent, and sharpening their skills all on their own. boxers do it because it's both simple and effective. It's where they sharpen their craft without needing anyone else. And that's exactly why we've adapted it to Rugby League. So, how does it work and how do you do it? Basically, you go down to a local field on your own and literally run through your sets. And when I say run through your set, I want you to do it just like this. You're going to pick a set type, the same type of set that your team would be using in a game from the point on your field that you're currently standing right now. Let's say that that set name is Rabbito, right? I want you to call the set name Rabbidos out loud enough that both wingers on opposite side of the field would be able to hear you if they were standing there. Next, you're going to call out the first play and you're going to be calling out the player's name that you want to be running that play. Let's say it's just a straight up hit up off the hooker and your team's name for that play is a drive. Okay, the player's name you want to run it is Henry and you want him taking it on the left side of the rock. So, you literally turn to where Henry would be standing and say, "Henry, drive left. Get there. Get there." and make sure to use the same volume in your voice that you should and you would be using in a game situation. And then you turn to your fullback. His name is Baz. Baz, push up with him. Push up with him. You've now you have the first play of the set sorted. Henry's taking a hit up and your fullback Baz is pushing up with him in support. Now, just as Henry and Baz are getting themselves into position for that first play, you're already moving further across the field for the second play of the set, which is a double X play. And your team's name for that is a double D. Because it's a double drop play, you'll take the ball off the hooker, drop a middle underneath, but you're going to dummy to him, straighten, then cut it 90° a second time, and you're going to hit the back rower on a second X. Now, just as Henry and Baz are about to run that first play, you're calling the next player, double D, double D. You're then telling the other front rower, his name is Sunny, and the second rower on that edge, his name is Jackson. You're telling those two players exactly what you want from them. Sunny, you've got the first D. Jackson, you've got the second. It's your ball. Get there, boys. Get there. a bit wider and a bit flatter. By this point, Henry has taken his hit up and he's about to get taken to the ground in the tackle. Your hooker, his name is Max, is right behind the rock, ready for a late offload, but also now looking in your direction for the next play. You make eye contact and say, "Yeah, Max, double D off me. Hit me early. Hit me early." Now, that hit me early call, it means that you don't want him to run. You don't want him to jump out and engage the markers, but you just want him to pass to you straight off the ground. No complications. A quick nod from Max tells you he's heard you and he understands. All right, now stop. Step back a minute. This is what shadow ball work looks like. It's you on your own running through your sets down at a footy field. You're moving around going through the exact motions with your feet, with your hands, and with your voice. If you're running that double Xplay, then you need to actually run that double Xplay at game pace. If you're organizing Henry and Baz to take that first hit up, then use their names. point to the actual place on the field that you want them to get to um for the start of the set and use the same volume in your voice that you need to use when you're in a game situation. And you know what? Yeah, you're going to feel like a little bit of an idiot running around on a field on your own calling out different sets, plays, and names, but it's up to you. If you want to get better, then this is the way to do it. And it works really well. Now, like I said a couple of times earlier, for you to do this properly, you need a high level of knowledge of the different set structures your team runs, the plays that exist within those set structures, and the players on your team. And often times, running the shadow ball work will help you understand where any gaps in your knowledge might be. As you stumble over the plays themselves or even even the names of, you know, your teammates that you want actually running those plays. And when you're going down the park and you're doing this, don't just do it for one set type. Get used to different scenarios from different points on the field using any and all set types that you have available to you. We're talking kickoff restarts, get out sets from close to your own line. We're talking from a 20 m tap kick, set restarts from a scrum, set restarts from a penalty tap kick in a good ball zone, and any and all possibilities. And that's how you do shadow ball work on your own running through your sets down at a footy field. And that's where everything we've been working on comes together. If you spend time there working through any gaps in your knowledge and ironing out the kinks in how you communicate and call plays, then when it comes to time to run ball work with your team at training, you'll feel confident and in control. Your leadership will be clear and your voice will carry weight. You'll be able to earn the respect of your teammates and guide them around the park the way a true halfback should. And when you get to the game itself, you'll be the one in the driver's seat making things happen. Trust me, because I'm Joe and Joe knows.

run into space and looking to combine and providing a Crossing up opportunity underlapping runs consist of the same concept except in these scenarios you are going to be making a run inside the field into the half Space versus running on the outside into the flanks sometimes the spaces in behind are already covered or you might have a teammate in that area so you're going to occupy the space inside to be an option inside or to create any other options for your teammates around that area lastly we have technical ability where it's your job to Showcase this in different areas of the field - 3 Ways To Become a Better Scrum Half - RugbySlate: Kind: captions Language: en the scrum half the number nine the link between the forwards and the backs everyone knows that they need to have a great pass a good level of fitness and a pinpoint box kick but outside of these core skills what else should a nine do before we start just a quick shameless plug if you're looking for a rugby related gift or something just for yourself please head over to shop.rugbyfate.com and check out our vintage rugby posters so let's get right into it three ways to become a better scrum half [Music] number one cheat lines this is an attacking concept as easy to miss if you're just a casual viewer of rugby but you might have wondered how come the player that primarily distributes the ball seems to crop up on the scoresheet so frequently the answer support line and more specifically cheat lines so what the hell is a cheat line well watch this phase by south africa the scrum half after clerk distributes the ball but then never takes a step backwards instead he predicts where the player is going to be and starts moving straight there this means that he's there to support and help gain those extra meters you can see from this above angle just how far ahead of the ball he is effectively cheating getting to where the ball is rather than just simply following the play in this example it doesn't lead to a try as opposed to this example with gloucester the scrum half gets quick ball from the first breakdown and identifies as an overlap out wide so arcs his run so he's in the perfect position to receive the pass and score a very easy try under the posts to add this to your game it's not just about blindly running an aggressive cheat line every phase it's about reading the play and making a good prediction the best way for a scrum half to bring this into their game is to focus on directly running sideways from a breakdown this gives them the option to curve it around and turn it into a cheat line but if there's a tackle at or behind the gain line you're still close enough to support number two defensive positioning if you see my video about the pendulum defense you'll be familiar with the idea of defending the backfield ready for a kick in that video i show this animation which doesn't actually tell you the full story a gap will open up behind the defensive line when the defenders move forwards towards the attackers this gap is the responsibility of the scrum half this might be obvious for some but is often overlooked not only does it stop short chips or grubber kicks but it also helps the defense in many other ways firstly the 9 will be able to spot any areas that need more defenders in the line and then fill them to stop the easy tries for the attack also by the scrum half being behind the line they're able to follow the play and make cover tackles if the attack do manage to get round the side of the defense and finally with the scrum half in this position you're given a second line of defense if there's any small breaks through the middle so the nine can quickly snuff them out defensive positioning for a scrum half is very difficult to coach as it's extremely dependent on the situation that is being faced the core principles to follow in order importance in something like this firstly do i need to defend the fringes of the breakdown if not do i need to join the defensive line to plug any holes and if not then i should fall into the typical position which will be about five to ten meters behind the defensive line following the ball across the field in reality it can end up being far more complicated than that number three sniping for those that don't know sniping is where the scrum half picks up from the base of a ruck or a mall and then runs with it themselves any decent nine will be constantly looking for gaps near the breakdown and attacking the fringes anytime there's an opportunity even forwards can get in on the action however what i want to talk about is where sniping is against defenders have a scrum half can use themselves to draw in players cause a mismatch or generate an overlap this is done by doing something that's typically avoided in rugby running sideways with the ball this can take a defender by surprise and if they're too slow to react can lead to an easy line break for the scrum half alternatively if a second defender is drawn in to tackle the 9 then a gap will likely open up and a simple offload will cause a big problem for the defense this concept can be very simple such as turning a one on one into a two on one by the nine simply picking up the ball and drawing the defender or it can be a bit more complicated but adding in switches to attack the gap left by the nine drawing the defenders across or even working into a pre-arranged move even if this sniping movement by the nine doesn't draw any mistakes out from the defense what it can do is give the 9 more time on the ball to make a better decision and pick the right option to get sniping to work the 9 wants to force the defense into making a decision and then exploiting any gap they leave even if that means getting flattened in the process the core idea behind this is to attack the inside shoulder of the outside defender here the 9 is focusing on the 6 so as the 9 runs across if the 6 is too focused on the 8 and comes up too fast the 9 can just easily go through this gap without a problem if the 3 is too slow at getting a cross so the six needs to help tackle the scrum half the nine can simply offload the ball to the eight for them to go through the gap or like we've seen before the eight can run a switch behind the line and exploit the newly created gap so there's three ways to become a better scrum half cheat lines defensive positioning and sniping if you found the animations helpful go check out rugbyslate on instagram for more play breakdowns of both forwards and backs moves if you want to make your own rugby animations head over to animator.rugbyslate.com and finally if you've enjoyed this video please consider subscribing for more and check out the other videos on the channel thanks very much

- How to PROPERLY Squat for Growth (4 Easy Steps): Kind: captions Language: en squats are the king of poorly performed exercises do them wrong and instead of growing nice quads and glutes you'll feel aches and pains in your knees your hips and even your shoulders but follow these four easy steps and I guarantee your squats will feel better and grow your muscles far better than they ever have before step one setup your Anatomy will determine what your perfect squat looks like so it's important to find the right setup for you and your body before you even get under the bar let's start with your footsteps hold the light weight at your chest set your feet hip width apart and squat down see how that feels and make note of how low you should get then widen it by an inch or two and compare even the slightest modification can make a world of difference for knee and hip pain so keep going until you find what feels best and enables you to squat the lowest as for your foot angle most people's joints will feel better with their toes pointed out slightly so don't force yourself to keep them straight and The Wider The Stance you use the more your toes will have to turn out once you've found the right stance hello there it's time to grab the bar set your feet under the bar bend your knees to get under it and then place the bar on your upper back but avoid placing it too high on the little bony bump at the base of your neck it should be placed right onto the meat of your traps next grip the bar with your hands as narrow as you can without feeling pain in your wrists shoulders or elbows the narrower they are the more upper back tightness and stability you'll have when you squat but if you have really tight shoulders or chest muscles you may have to widen this once you grab the bar Walk It Out by extending your legs to lift it out of the rack and then taking three careful steps take a little step back with one foot a little step back with your second foot and then use the third step to widen or adjust your feet to your ideal squat stance lastly to keep you balanced as you squat spread your weight evenly over your foot by making sure each foot has three solid points of contact with the ground your big toe your pinky toe and your heel you're now ready for step two creating whole body tension now at this point you do not want to just squat down you need to First activate several key muscles that will protect your back and keep you strong as you squat down failure to do this is why so many people have a really strong leg press but I have a much weaker and unstable squat what start with the lower body screw your feet into the ground as if you were trying to touch your heels together then level your pelvis with the floor by tilting it back as if it was a bowl of water and you were trying to prevent it from spilling once adjusted lock this in by squeezing your inner thighs glutes and quads for the upper body avoid pointing your elbows back and losing tightness in your upper back instead pull your elbows forward under the bar and pull the bar down into your media upper traps as if you're trying to bend the bar in half this helps activate your lats and keeps your upper back stable finally take a deep 360 breath into your waist as if you're trying to expand all sides of a weight belt and then brace your core as if you just coughed really hard at this point you should feel your legs core and lats all working you're ready for step three The Descent pull yourself down into the squat using your hamstrings let your butt drop down between your heels and push your knees out in the same direction as your toes keep your upper back tight by pulling the bar down and continue lowering as deep as you comfortably can as for how deep you should go a 2019 study assigned one group of subjects to half squats and another group to deep squats twice a week for 10 weeks although both groups had similar growth in the quads the glutes and abductors grew significantly more in the Deep squat group so your goal should be to squat down to at least somewhere close to parallel if you use a narrower stance you should be able to get a little bit deeper if you use a wider stance it'll be closer to parallel but if towards the bottom of your squat your upper body falls forward or your heels come off the ground or your lower back rounds excessively into Butt Wink these are all signs that you're not yet ready to go that deep and there's two main reasons for this the most common reason is the limited ankle mobility in this case try elevating your heels onto weight plates and see if that helps the other reason is a weak core and glutes in this case you can try out a more stable exercise like Smith machine squats before progressing to the barbell but once you can get deep enough you may notice that your knees end up over your toes this is commonly believed to be harmful on the knees one study tested this by using a wooden barrier to prevent the knees from going over the toes while there was a bit less pressure on the knees when compared to letting them travel over the toes the stress on the hips and lower back significantly increased so as long as your knees don't cave in or out and instead push out in the same direction as your toes letting your knees go over your toes is perfectly fine now once you reach that bottom position do not just let gravity drop you into that last introduce research suggests this is the most important part of the movement for growth so force your muscles to work by controlling the weight down and pausing at that bottom position for half a second before moving on to step four the ascent but first just keep in mind that everyone's bodies are different to maximize growth and prevent injury it's important that you find the right exercises and the right form for your individual structure and for step-by-step plan that takes care of all the guesswork for you after this video just head on over to builtwithscience.com and take our free analysis quiz to find the best program for you and your body but for now let's move on to step four the ascent the most common mistake people make here is shooting their hips back and letting their upper body fall forward this puts a lot of pressure on the lower back we want the chest and hips to rise at the same rate to do this drive up out of the Bottom by pushing the floor away and thinking about driving your traps up against the bar keep your glutes engaged by keeping your knees in line with your toes rather than keeping them in continue pushing down against the floor and getting tall until your legs are fully extended but as you come to the top avoid letting out a big exhale instead we want to maximize the pressure in your abdomen to protect your back and you can do this by keeping your lips held tighter together as you breathe out to gradually let air out once you've let all the air out take another deep 360 breath in brace and repeat for another rep congrats my friend you have just done the perfect squat you can give this video a watch next to learn how to properly deadlift without hurting your back and I'll see you next time

  10. @HarmanHundal01: ### Summary of the 4 Steps

1.  Setup (0:20 - 2:13):
•	Foot Stance: Experiment with width and angle to find what feels best for your joints and allows for maximum depth (0:29). The wider the stance, the more toes will need to turn out (1:02). 
•	Bar Placement: Position the bar on the meaty part of your traps, not your neck (1:08). Place feet under the bar, half-squat to get under it (1:11). 
The narrower you hold the bar i.e the closer your hands are to your torso the more the stability under you. If your chest or shoulder muscles are really tight then hold it wider.
•	Grip & Walkout: Grip as narrowly as comfortable for stability (1:24), take three steps back, and balance weight across your foot (three points of contact: big toe, pinky toe, heel) (2:01). 

2.  Whole Body Tension (2:13 - 3:28):
•	Lower Body: "Screw" your feet into the floor as if trying to touch heels together, level the pelvis, and brace glutes and quads (2:33). 
•	Upper Body: Pull elbows forward and pull the bar down to activate lats and create back stability (2:55). 
•	Core: Take a deep 360-degree breath into your waist and brace hard (3:12). 

3.  Descent (3:28 - 6:08):
•	Execution: Pull yourself down using hamstrings, let your butt drop down between heels, and push knees out in the same direction as toes (3:31). 
•	Depth: Aim for at least parallel, or deeper if mobility allows (3:49). If you experience "butt wink" or heels lifting, improve ankle mobility (use heel elevation) or strengthen the core/glutes (4:28).  If you use a narrower stance you will be able to go deeper. If you use a woder stance you'd remain a little parellal.
•	Knees & Control: Let knees travel over toes if necessary, but do not let them cave in (4:54). Control the descent and pause for half a second at the bottom for maximum growth (5:27). 
Pause at the bottom for half a second. This is very important.

4.  Ascent (6:08 - 7:16):
•	Drive: Push the floor away and drive your traps up against the bar (6:23). 
•	Technique: Keep the chest and hips rising together and keep glutes engaged (6:12). 
•	Breathing: Gradually exhale through tight lips to maintain abdominal pressure and protect your back (6:44).

Ran a 70 yardd touchdown, his first run ever in pee-wee football. >> Gives me all the confidence that I need to go out there and just be Jeremiah Love. >> Jeremiah Love with the hurdle. Love breaks free trying to go 98. Touchdown. >> When you see Jeremiah Love on the field, there's little doubt the Notre Dame running back is a gifted and unique talent. But even before football, Jeremiah knew he was different. >> Coming up as a kid, I guess you could say odd really. Like I did things on my own. Didn't really have any friends growing up. He kept to himself a lot. So, he was pretty adventurous. >> That's what I'm talking about. >> But when we started school, we start to notice certain things about Jeremiah that was a little different. There's different mannerisms, his attention to detail. If his shoes weren't symmetrically placed in order, he would be mad. My clothes definitely had to fit a certain way. Particularly my pants. I never liked my pants to like touch my ankles. Like it felt really weird. It took us 20 minutes to get him ready for school because he would just become dysfunctional. >> At first I just thought he was being difficult and then I realized he couldn't concentrate if things weren't just the way he liked them. >> We were going crazy like what is wrong with our baby? Jeremiah's parents spent years trying to manage their son's distinctive mannerisms until finally a breakthrough. >> Sports was definitely something my parents used to, you know, help me with some of those tendencies. >> Anything competitive was his trigger. It would drive him for excellence. >> Once we identified that, we were able to say, "Hey, this guy's better than you doing this. From then on, like that competitive drive, you know, helped me to really excel. >> Competing against people was one thing, but for Jeremiah, interacting with them was another. >> He would tell me, "Ma, I don't feel emotion." He was like, "He didn't feel happiness. He didn't feel grief. He didn't feel none of that." I was like, "Yeah, you do." He was like, "No, I don't." Jeremiah was perceived to be at times standoffish, a loner, not a team player. >> When he started playing sports on a higher level, we knew that that was going to bleed over into the coaches. So, that was the main reason we went to the doctor cuz we wanted to get a diagnosis. We spoke with the pediatrician who had been seeing Jeremiah since he was a baby and he kind of leaned into that Jeremiah could be on the spectrum that he could be higher functioning. That kind of crushed me and mom cuz we was like, "Hey, we've been chastising him for a while, trying to make him do certain things and he just saw the world different." >> Jeremiah's parents chose not to pursue an official diagnosis. >> The doctor advised us, "Just continue to let him be who he is and learn how to adapt to it and encourage him. Don't try to change him. let him change the world. >> I always kind of knew I had some tendencies, but it didn't really affect me because I'm totally accept the type of person that I am. I feel like that particularness about myself is, you know, helped me to, you know, be a better football player. It gives me a lot of power over my own life. We refuse to label him. So, we had to relay that message to the coaches without any medical evidence saying that this is what it is. But his talent was becoming undeniable. >> In 2023, after emerging as one of the nation's best high school running backs, Jeremiah took his talents to Southbend. >> When I first met him, quiet, unique personality. His father was the one that said, "Hey, coach, he might be different than everybody, but Jeremiah, you got to let him be Jeremiah. Jeremiah Love would not be denied. One of the most relentless runs you'll ever see. >> Every time he does something amazing and I think about what the doctor said, I think we did a good job of allowing Jeremiah to be who he was going to be. >> Now he's a shining example for all to see that it's not a weakness. That's his superpower. My parents never made me feel like I was odd. Some will probably think I'm crazy, but feel like for that certain group of people, they'll feel inspired. They'll feel connected and they'll feel welcome to be who they are. >> You want this, you got that. No way. Watch them walk right past you. Run fast. Run fast while you have the chance. Don't speak.

- How Culture Drives Behaviours | Julien S. Bourrelle | TEDxTrondheim: Kind: captions Language: en Transcriber: Tijana Mihajlović Reviewer: Ilze Garda I was in Brussels. I was sitting on La Grand-Place, which is a beautiful square in the center of the town. Suddenly, a man came and sat next to me, and started talking to me, so I turned to him and I answered. Then I turned back and I asked myself, "Why is he talking to me?" Suddenly, I realized, "Julian, you're becoming Norwegian." (Laughter) So I turned to the man and I said, "Sorry, I live in a country where people don't speak to each other." The thing is, in Norway, it is not that people don't speak to each other; it's that socialization takes part in a much more framed and organized manner. I was not expecting this man as a stranger to come and talk to me. However, this is surprising, because I come from the French-speaking part of Canada where that type of behavior is totally normal. However, my mental programming has changed. My brain has been rewired, because during the last five years I've lived in a tiny little country in the north of Europe which is called Norway. When you move to a different country, there are three ways that you can relate to the culture: you can confront, complain, or conform. When you confront, you believe that your behaviors are the right behaviors. When you complain, what happens is that you will isolate yourself into social bubbles of foreigners living in segregation with the society. When you adapt your way to behave, when you conform to the whole society, then you can truly benefit from diversity. But that implies that you are observing, learning, understanding the behaviors of others, and adapting your own, so that it fits with the behaviors of the society you're in. I was in the north-east of Spain, in a beautiful region of Catalonia, and I was there with a very good friend of mine. He is two-meters tall, blond hair, and blue eyes. We were visiting the beautiful region where they're making the cava, the Spanish sparkling wine. After the guided tour, we asked some more questions to the very charming guide that was there, and she was explaining us with passion about what she was doing, and then suddenly she stopped. She took a step aside, she took my friend, and she shook him. And then she looked at me and said, "Why is he not interested in what I'm saying?" Because she was not getting the emotional feedback she was used to. She was seeing his emotional feedback through her own cultural glasses, meaning that she was interpreting the fact that he had a neutral face on what it would mean if someone from her culture would have that face, and that would mean that the person was not interested or didn't want to be there. And we all see the world through cultural glasses. The lens through which your brain sees the world shapes your reality. If you can change the lens, not only can you change the way your brain perceives behaviors, but you can change the way people relate to cultural differences. Embedded within that statement is the key to benefiting from diversity. Three years ago, I was sitting on the board of directors of one major university in Northern Europe and I was representing 2,000 academic staff, and I wanted to become a better leader. So I've looked around the whole university for a leadership class that would be suited to my position, and I found one, and I was thrilled, because not only would I learn about leadership, but because I would also learn about how women lead, because the class was called "Leadership for women". And so, as naive as I was, I've registered for the class. The next morning, the gender equality adviser of the university calls me and says, "Julian, this is leadership for women. You're a man. You cannot attend." It was the first time in my life that I was denied education based on my gender. This is my cultural perspective about what happened there. However, why is the university doing this? Because the government had been putting in place a scheme that allowed the university to take candidates in full academic position before someone that has higher academic training if the candidates can document leadership training. By offering leadership training only to women, the university was fast forwarding the track of women into full professorship position at a place where less than 20% of women had professorship. I call this equality of result. Not equality of opportunity; equality of results. I did not have the same opportunity to flourish to my full potential, but the result is that we have a balance in society. We enforce diversity, and there is a good reason to do this. Studies show that boards composed of both genders will perform 15% better then boards that are composed of mainly one gender. But studies also show that boards that are composed of different cultures will perform 35% better than boards that are composed of only one culture. Cultural diversity increases problem-solving ability. It increases creativity and innovation. The real challenge here is to make people being able to communicate well together. And this you do through explaining cultural differences. Two years ago, I was sitting in my living room. I was sitting there with a friend and we started to draw typical cultural situations. Then we made a Facebook page, and then we made a free website, and then I started to lecture all around the country. I'm happy to say we've just crossed one million people that have seen these drawings to help to connect culture. And the idea behind that project is to create a simple, humoristic way in connecting people of different cultures, especially in Norway. You know that most people around the world are raised with the idea that they will need to contribute to a group, that they will be part of a group and interdependent on their members. And it affects the way people behave. Other parts of the world, especially the Western world, we raise our children to be independent and to be self-sufficient, and we create certain independence in society, and it changes behaviors. You see the difference? This basic principle tells a lot about how you're going to expect a friendship to look like. In certain societies where the group prevails, the friendship will be much stronger, in terms that people will live in symbiosis with each other and dependent on each other, and they will be expected to be invited to every single event that the very good friend will do. However, in other cultures, friendship will be much more distant. I've asked a Scandinavian man one day what a good friend was. You know what he answered? "It is someone I can sit in silence in a room and feel comfortable." If you tell this to a South American, they won't understand what the principle is. This is about friendship and love, and contact with people is one of the six basic human needs. If you're not able to see how this friendship and love is communicated to you because you are blinded by your cultural glasses, you will spend years believing you have no friends. You will spend years believing that people are rejecting you. It is about changing these cultural glasses. This is when you know that a Norwegian bus stop is full and that you need to stand. What happens if you sit in the middle? It could very well be that one of the two persons stands up, takes a step aside, starts playing on his phone. Now, what if you look different? What if you're wearing a religious symbol? How easy it is to believe that the person has moved away because you're of a different skin color or of a different religion? A typical cultural misunderstanding and a very basic of human interactions: you've came into the personal space of someone who has a much bigger personal space. In most cultures in the world, there's place for 4 people on that bench. Not understanding these very subtle physical differences with people will actually lead to lot of miscommunication. If you want to observe it yourself, go to any international conference and try to observe a South American that tries to communicate with a North European. What will happen there is that the South American will be very eager and will stand at a distance that's comfortable for him. The North European will be also very eager, but stand a little bit further away, because he's not comfortable that the South American is so close. If you observe it over time, you will see that a little dance starts and people go around the room, none of them realizing that they are feeling uncomfortable, or they both feel uncomfortable, but they don't realize why. It's just a simple thing of culture and being able to feel that distance between people, which is different in every culture. And that has to do as well with politeness. Politeness is a concept which is very much culturally related. It's a group of norms and social codes that everyone obeys to, so that communication goes well in the society, and in certain societies it is very strict, and you have a way to talk, and you have a way to behave. You change the way - you're changing the words in the sentence. In other places, politeness might only mean not to disturb others, to leave more space, both in friendship and physical space. And if you move to another country and no one explains you what politeness means, how can you expect people - how can you expect that someone will behave as he's expected to in a foreign culture? The key here is to benefit from diversity. Everyone sees the world It's not about what you see; it's about what you perceive. And it is by taking small steps that we will one day help the world to truly benefit from diversity. Thank you. (Applause)
